_comment: Research portfolio: the small set of LIVE ideas, not a brainstorm dump. Prune aggressively -- an idea leaves via `validated` or `dropped`, and its conclusion lives in out/hypothesis*.tsv. `causal_distance` is measured from the CURRENT lineage (decoder/attention tweaks on RayDPT): near = same family, mid = different component of the same pipeline, far = different abstraction level or transferred from another field. Statuses: backlog | probing | candidate | verifying | validated | dropped | inconclusive.

ideas.origin: physical property of the sensing modality (measured, not searched)
ideas.target_bottleneck: time-of-flight quantisation in the input representation
ideas.general_hypothesis: Depth from echoes is fundamentally a time-of-flight measurement: a surface at distance d returns its echo at t = 2d/c. If the input representation cannot resolve t finely, no decoder -- however expressive -- can resolve d. The current pipeline may be starved of temporal information long before the model sees it.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: CORRECTED (before any GPU was spent): the STFT's temporal RESOLUTION is set by the analysis WINDOW, not by the hop. The window support smears an echo over c*win/(2*sr) of one-way depth; the hop only sets how densely that smeared function is sampled. At the default win=400 (8.33ms) the smear is 1.417 m -- essentially equal to the achieved RMSE of 1.3207 m, which is why the window is the suspect. Shrinking the hop alone (160->40) leaves the smear at 1.417 m and merely oversamples. To sharpen time-of-flight one must shorten the WINDOW (win 400 -> 64 gives a 0.227 m smear), paying frequency resolution for it.
ideas.original_detailed_hypothesis_WRONG: Earlier version claimed hop=160 'quantises depth at 0.567m' and that shrinking the hop adds information. The hop quantum is real but is NOT the resolution limit; the window is. Kept here so the correction is auditable.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: RMSE falls (range error), NOT d1. Per D3 the interaural cues already buy d1 (azimuth); if a temporal change buys d1 instead of RMSE, it is not acting through time-of-flight.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: Neither arm improves RMSE -> temporal resolution is not the binding constraint, DROP. If BOTH arms improve equally, the gain is extra sampling density / capacity, not resolution, and the mechanism story is wrong even if the score moves.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: Two arms on run_base.py (cheapest parent), control = E2 (win 400, hop 160, smear 1.417m). A: win 400, hop 40 -> T=71, smear UNCHANGED 1.417m (density only). B: win 64, hop 16 -> T=177, smear 0.227m (true resolution). The design DISSOCIATES sampling density from resolution: only B should improve RMSE if I1 is right.
ideas.prior_weakened_by: A training-free nearest-neighbour retrieval probe (400 train / 120 val) found hop=40 no better than hop=160 (composite 3.048 vs 3.016). But that instrument is INVALID -- its NN retrieval (rmse 2.20) is worse than the trivial mean-training-depth predictor (rmse 1.64), so it cannot detect anything. It is recorded as a failed instrument, not as evidence against I1. Separately, a quantisation argument cuts against the ORIGINAL framing: a 0.567m quantum implies ~0.16m RMSE if quantisation were binding, while the achieved RMSE is 1.32m. That arithmetic is what exposed the window/hop confusion.
ideas.budget_risk: low-medium. Model FLOPs/VRAM/in_ch unchanged (feature is resized to (256,512) regardless). But win=64,hop=16 yields T=177 frames -> more STFT work per sample, so dataloading is slower and fewer epochs fit the wall-clock budget. Report epochs_ran; per D2 epoch count is a confound.
ideas.evidence_required: Arm B (win 64) must improve RMSE while arm A (hop only) does not. A d1-only gain does NOT count (per D3, d1 is the cue-driven metric).
ideas.unblocked_by: The STFT analysis window is now editable research logic (--stft-nfft/--stft-hop/--stft-win) read from cfg.dataset. Defaults verified bit-identical; changing them leaves cut/depth/in_ch/shape untouched.
ideas.strengthened_by: (a) I2's oracle diagnostic: the decoder's 64x128 output resolution imposes a ceiling of composite 0.2399 while the model sits at 1.8567 -- the bottleneck is NOT downstream of the decoder, so it is upstream: information. (b) The S0 2x2 dissociation: interaural cues buy d1 (angle) and log1p buys rmse (range), yet the best rmse is still 1.3088 m -- and the analysis window smears an echo over 1.417 m of one-way depth, essentially the same size. Range error and window smear coincide.
ideas.next_action: HOLD. Do not run more I1 arms. I10 (bilinear at hop=160) isolates the staircase; interpret this lineage only after it lands.
ideas.arm_A_result: E5 (win400 hop40, smear UNCHANGED 1.417m): composite 1.8514 vs control E2 1.8646, delta +0.0132 ABOVE sigma~0.008. rmse -0.0227 (1.3207 -> 1.2980), d1 -0.0019 (flat), abs_rel -0.0089. The arm designed to show NO effect improved RMSE -- the exact metric I1 predicted. Per the pre-registered rule this WEAKENS the resolution story: sampling density alone moves range accuracy.
ideas.arm_B_result: E6 (win64 hop16, smear 0.227m, a 6.2x resolution increase): composite 1.8379 -- the best batvision run so far -- but the gain is in d1 (+0.0067 vs E2) and abs_rel, NOT in RMSE. Its RMSE (1.3114) is WORSE than arm A's (1.2980), which had no resolution increase at all.
ideas.verdict: MECHANISM AS STATED: UNSUPPORTED. The pre-registered prediction was 'B improves RMSE, A does not'. The opposite happened: A (density only) improved RMSE by 0.0227 while B (true resolution) improved d1 and left RMSE worse than A. Per D3's rule -- a time-of-flight gain must appear in RANGE, not ANGLE -- sharpening temporal resolution does NOT act through time-of-flight range accuracy. Both arms improved the composite, so the SCORE moved while the MECHANISM story failed: exactly the case program.md warns to separate.
ideas.confound: E6 changes THREE things at once versus E2: temporal smear 1.417 -> 0.227 m, staircase block 28.4 -> 2.9 px, and frequency resolution (win 400 -> 64) is sacrificed. Its result cannot be attributed to any one of them. The composite happens to be MONOTONE in staircase fineness across all three runs (28.4px -> 1.8646, 7.2px -> 1.8514, 2.9px -> 1.8379), which is why I10 -- which varies the staircase ALONE, holding information and window fixed -- is now the decisive experiment for this whole lineage.
ideas.scope_of_negative_evidence: Refutes the RANGE mechanism for temporal resolution on the batvision reference under this budget and metric. It does NOT refute that the representation matters -- both arms beat the control. It does not test RayDPT, and it does not rule out that a shorter window helps for a reason unrelated to time-of-flight (e.g. the staircase, or transient sharpening that aids azimuth cues).

ideas.origin: measured property of the planar target (grad-energy, 24 samples / 6 scenes)
ideas.target_bottleneck: (claimed) output resolution: 4x bilinear upsample smears the target's sharp steps
ideas.general_hypothesis: The planar target is piecewise-constant, so a decoder that cannot represent a step cannot fit it, and d1 punishes blurred edges. RayDPT decodes at 64x128 and upsamples 4x; add a 128x256 scale to halve that.
ideas.why_dropped: REFUTED by an oracle diagnostic that cost zero GPU time. Downsample the GROUND TRUTH to each decode resolution and bilinearly upsample it back: that is what a PERFECT predictor at that resolution would score. At RayDPT's 64x128 the oracle scores d1=0.9757, RMSE=0.2873m, composite=0.2399. The best ACHIEVED is d1=0.5938, RMSE=1.3207m, composite=1.8646. The model is nowhere near the ceiling its decode resolution imposes -- roughly 1.62 composite of headroom remains. Raising the ceiling to 128x256 (oracle composite 0.1638) buys nothing the model can currently use.
ideas.scope_of_negative_evidence: Refutes decoder output resolution as the CURRENT binding constraint, at the present error level, for this target and metric. It does NOT say a finer decode is worthless forever: if a future model approaches d1 ~ 0.9, the 64x128 ceiling becomes real and this idea should be revived. Recorded as a ceiling to re-check, not a closed door.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: (would have been) d1 rises
ideas.next_action: none. Revive only if achieved d1 approaches ~0.9.

ideas.origin: unexplained result from E0/E1 (best at epoch 14 of 26, then 12 epochs of overfitting)
ideas.target_bottleneck: the 1h wall-clock budget is spent on epochs that make the model worse
ideas.general_hypothesis: A schedule sized for a different epoch count is not a schedule. The cosine LR is written for --epochs 40 but only ~26 fit the wall-clock budget, so LR never anneals and training ends mid-schedule at a degraded point, while the last ~12 epochs actively overfit.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: Set --epochs to what actually fits (~24-26) so cosine anneals to zero inside the budget. The annealed tail is where most models make their final, best-generalising progress; recovering it should improve the composite at zero extra compute.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: composite improves; the best epoch moves to near the LAST epoch instead of epoch 14. If the best epoch stays early, the cause is overfitting, not the schedule, and weight decay / EMA is the right lever instead.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: with an annealed schedule the best epoch is still ~14/26 -- the model overfits regardless of LR, so the schedule was never the problem.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: one run of the E1 config with --epochs 26. Compare best_epoch and composite.
ideas.evidence_required: best_epoch moves late AND composite improves beyond sigma=0.008.
ideas.next_action: queue after the RayDPT planar re-anchor (E4); this is a confound affecting EVERY future run, so fix it early

ideas.origin: own reasoning, refuted before consuming any GPU time
ideas.target_bottleneck: (claimed) the net wastes capacity learning cube-edge creases
ideas.general_hypothesis: planar = radial * linf with linf = max(|dx|,|dy|,|dz|) a fixed per-pixel field; predict the smooth radial field and multiply by the known geometry so the creases come for free.
ideas.why_dropped: The premise is measurably false. Grad-energy over 24 samples / 6 scenes: planar 0.01618, radial 0.01677; radial is smoother in only 17% of samples (mean ratio 1.035). PLANAR is already the smoother parameterisation -- for an axis-aligned room a wall perpendicular to a cube face has CONSTANT planar depth, while radial curves as 1/cos. Reparameterising to radial would make the regression HARDER.
ideas.scope_of_negative_evidence: Refutes THIS reparameterisation under the planar target on SoundSpaces rooms (mostly axis-aligned). It says nothing about geometry-conditioned heads in general, and nothing about scenes with non-axis-aligned walls. Do not generalise it into 'geometric conditioning does not help'.
ideas.next_action: none. Revive only with new evidence (e.g. a non-axis-aligned benchmark).

ideas.origin: reading the code against the qualitative figure (predictions show quasi-periodic azimuthal banding)
ideas.target_bottleneck: RayDPT's DPT skip connections impose a FALSE spatial correspondence between the spectrogram's axes and the ERP's axes
ideas.general_hypothesis: A skip connection asserts that a pixel at (i,j) in the encoder feature means the same thing as the pixel at (i,j) in the output. That assertion is only valid when input and output share a coordinate system. Here they do not: the input's height is FREQUENCY and its width is TIME, while the output's height is ELEVATION and its width is AZIMUTH. Adding one to the other by pixel index is a category error, and it may be actively injecting structure that has no geometric meaning.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: _features() produces (C, F=257, T=18) and interpolates it to (C,256,512): height <- frequency, width <- time (nearest-upsampled 28.4x, so the width axis is a staircase of 18 constant blocks). RayDPT then does `m16 = F16 + se4(e4)` and `up(m16) + F32 + se3(e3)` -- adding encoder features to the ray-query grid at matching pixel coordinates. This ties azimuth to echo arrival TIME and elevation to FREQUENCY. The ray-conditioned cross-attention (kv_e4/kv_e3) does NOT have this problem: it is global, so it only sees an unordered token set and can learn any mapping. The DPT skips are therefore the one place where a false geometry is hard-coded. Predicted signature: the predicted depth maps should show azimuthal banding with a period tied to the 18-block staircase (~28.4 px at full width, ~7.1 px at e2's 128-wide grid), which is visible in out/display/qualitative.png.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: Removing or re-routing the spatial skips should reduce azimuthal banding. d1 (angular assignment) should improve; RMSE may be unchanged. If banding persists without the skips, the false correspondence was not its cause.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: An FFT of the predicted depth along azimuth shows NO peak at the 18-block period, i.e. the banding is not tied to the staircase -- then the story is wrong even if ablating skips helps for other reasons.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: DIAGNOSTIC FIRST, zero GPU: take the existing batvision predictions and FFT them along the azimuth axis; check for a spectral peak at period ~28.4 px (or its harmonics) that is absent in the GT's azimuthal spectrum. Only if the peak exists is the architecture change worth an hour. NOTE the diagnostic works on batvision too -- it also consumes the same (freq,time) image -- so it tests the REPRESENTATION's staircase, not RayDPT's skips. To isolate the skips, a second arm ablating se2/se3/se4 on RayDPT is needed.
ideas.budget_risk: low for the diagnostic (no GPU). Medium for the ablation: removing skips changes RayDPT's parameter count and may need its own re-tune.
ideas.evidence_required: an azimuthal spectral peak at the staircase period in predictions but not in GT; then a RayDPT skip-ablation that reduces it.
ideas.next_action: none. Do not spend GPU on the skip ablation on this rationale. Revive only with an independent reason.
ideas.diagnostic_result: Pre-registered signature ABSENT. Azimuthal FFT of E3 batvision predictions (24 val samples): at the staircase frequency k=18 (period 28.4 px) the local prominence is 0.806 in PRED (a dip, not a peak) vs 1.041 in GT; pred/gt power ratio at k=18 is 0.05, the same as its neighbours k=17,19. No harmonic peaks at k=36/54 either. The nearest-upsampled 18-block staircase is NOT reproduced in the output.
ideas.scope_of_negative_evidence: Refutes the predicted SIGNATURE (staircase banding), measured on batvision -- which also mixes encoder features into the decoder by pixel index via U-Net skips, so the absence of the peak is real evidence against 'pixel-index skips inject the staircase'. It does NOT test RayDPT's DPT skips directly, and it does not refute the underlying axis-mismatch concern, which now has NO supporting evidence and no cheap test. Per the pre-registered rule, the story as stated is wrong even if a skip ablation later helps for other reasons. Parked, not pursued.

ideas.origin: metric decomposition + azimuthal spectrum of predictions (zero GPU)
ideas.target_bottleneck: the objective devotes most of its gradient to low-frequency terms, so the model may be trained to be blurry
ideas.general_hypothesis: A model fits what the loss measures. If most of the training signal comes from deliberately low-frequency views of the prediction, the model has little incentive to produce high-frequency structure, and will converge to a low-pass answer even when the target is sharp.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: composite_loss = 1.0*dense_MAE + 1.0*coarse_layout(16x32) + 0.5*low_pass(sigma=3). MEASURED at E3's convergence (mae 0.0392, lc 0.0360, llow 0.0370): the two low-frequency auxiliaries contribute 58.2% of the total loss (coarse 38.4%, low-pass 19.7%), leaving only 41.8% to the full-resolution dense term. program.md states lc/llow are auxiliary regularisers, free to tune or zero. Zeroing them should let the dense term dominate and restore high-frequency azimuthal content.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: Predicted azimuthal power above k=6 rises toward GT's; d1 improves (blur is what a +-25% threshold metric punishes). If d1 falls, the auxiliaries were load-bearing regularisers, not a bias.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: With w_coarse_layout=0 and w_low=0 the predicted azimuthal spectrum still collapses to k<=6. Then the objective is NOT the cause of the low-pass behaviour, and I7 (binaural angular under-determination) is the surviving explanation.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: one run of run_base.py (cheapest parent, E3 config) with --w-coarse-layout 0 --w-low 0; recompute the azimuthal spectrum with the same diagnostic. Requires exposing the two loss weights as CLI flags (they are currently hard-coded in make_config).
ideas.evidence_required: a measurable rise in predicted azimuthal power for k>6, together with a d1 that does not regress.
ideas.next_action: none. Optionally delete the aux terms for simplicity (neutral).
ideas.why_dropped: REFUTED. Zeroing BOTH low-frequency auxiliaries -- 58.2% of the gradient at convergence -- changed almost nothing. E7 (aux off) composite 1.8613 vs E3 (aux on) 1.8567: delta +0.0046, BELOW sigma~0.008. The azimuthal spectrum barely moved: high-frequency power (k>=7) went from 10.8% of GT's to 12.3%. Removing more than half the training signal did not un-blur the model. The objective is NOT the cause of the low-pass behaviour.
ideas.secondary_finding: The two auxiliaries are INERT, not harmful: they consume 58% of the loss and buy nothing measurable. Classification: redundant mechanism (neutral result). They are therefore a free simplification candidate -- program.md: a gain from deleting code is the best kind -- but deleting them is a cosmetic change, not a research result.
ideas.scope_of_negative_evidence: Refutes the objective as the cause of low-pass behaviour for the batvision reference, at this error level, under this budget. It does not say auxiliary losses are useless in general, and it does not test them on RayDPT.

ideas.origin: physics of the sensor, prompted by the azimuthal spectrum
ideas.target_bottleneck: two microphones may fundamentally under-determine high azimuthal frequencies
ideas.general_hypothesis: Azimuth is recovered only from interaural differences between TWO receivers. A two-element array has a broad, smooth directional response, so fine angular structure in the scene maps onto almost identical binaural cues. If so, high azimuthal frequencies are not merely hard to learn -- they are close to unobservable, and no loss or decoder can conjure them.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: MEASURED: the predicted azimuthal power spectrum holds 97.4% of its non-DC power in k<=6 while GT holds 75.8%; the model over-represents k=4 (pred/gt 1.94), which corresponds to the four walls of a room -- genuine coarse geometry -- and under-represents everything finer (pred/gt ~0.05 for k>=17). The cutoff k~6 corresponds to ~60 degrees of azimuth, a plausible effective beamwidth for a two-element array.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: If true, d1 is capped by angular observability, and the productive levers are the REPRESENTATION (cues that carry more angular information) rather than the decoder or the loss.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: I6's ablation restores high-frequency azimuthal content. Then the low-pass behaviour was an artefact of the objective, not of the sensor, and I7 is dropped.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: I6's ablation is the discriminating experiment for BOTH ideas: it cleanly separates 'the loss made it blurry' from 'the sensor cannot see it'. Run I6 first; I7 needs no separate run to be tested.
ideas.evidence_required: survives if and only if I6's ablation fails to restore high-frequency content.
ideas.next_action: Do not chase high-frequency power as a goal. Re-test the observability claim once RayDPT converges (E7), using I11's contrast.
ideas.support: Survives by elimination, per the pre-registered rule. The low-pass character is ROBUST to (a) a 58% change in the objective (I6: high-freq 10.8% -> 12.3% of GT) and (b) a 6.2x change in temporal resolution (E6). Two very different interventions upstream and downstream both fail to restore fine azimuthal structure, which is what an observability limit looks like. FURTHER: I11 refuted the bottleneck explanation. Low-pass behaviour now survives (a) a 58% change to the objective, (b) a 6.2x change in temporal resolution, and (c) removing the network's information waist entirely. Three independent interventions, upstream and downstream, none of which restores fine azimuthal structure.
ideas.caveat_strong_form: The STRONG form -- 'high azimuthal frequencies are unobservable, so recovering them is the goal' -- is itself now suspect, because E6 has 29.7% LESS high-frequency power than E2 yet a BETTER d1 (0.6005 vs 0.5938). Sharpness and d1 are not the same thing: d1 rewards each pixel landing within +-25% of truth, which a well-centred smooth field can do better than a wrongly-placed sharp one. So 'restore high frequencies' may be the wrong objective entirely.
ideas.strengthened_by: DECISIVE: D13 resolved sensor-limited. FIVE echo-delay-time-resolution runs (E24/E27/E29/E30/E31) saturate past the first dose, and E31 bypassing the encoder entirely still regressed the far field. Time resolution is exhausted as a far-field lever; what remains is angular/range observability with two mics, which is exactly I7.

ideas.origin: E4, the first RayDPT run under the planar target
ideas.target_bottleneck: RayDPT is COMPUTE-STARVED under the fixed 1-hour wall-clock budget
ideas.general_hypothesis: When the budget is wall-clock, throughput IS accuracy. A model that fits 5 epochs cannot be compared with one that fits 25; the slower model is penalised for its speed, not judged on its mechanism. Before any RayDPT mechanism can be evaluated, RayDPT must be able to converge inside the budget.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: E4 ran 713.5 s/epoch vs batvision's ~130 s, so only 5 epochs fit against batvision's 25. Its best checkpoint is the LAST epoch and every val metric was still improving monotonically (rmse 1.485->1.424->1.399, d1 0.513->0.530->0.542). So E4's composite 2.0471 vs E3's 1.8567 measures budget, not architecture. MEASURED module cost (CPU forward, indicative): the 64x128 scale dominates -- lsa64 29.3% + cross64 27.7% = 57% of forward. LocalSphericalAttention builds its neighbourhood with F.unfold, materialising (B, C*win*win, H*W): 1.8 GB at 64x128 for k and v at batch 16, which also explains the 16.2 GB peak VRAM. Levers, in order of expected value/risk: bf16 autocast; replace the unfold in _window_kv with a gather/shift-based neighbourhood; drop or cheapen the 64-scale cross-attention.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: epochs_ran rises toward batvision's; the composite improves BECAUSE the model reaches convergence, not because the mechanism changed. Report epochs_ran alongside every number.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: Throughput improves but the composite does not, AND the best epoch is again the last -- then RayDPT is not merely starved and its per-epoch learning is genuinely weaker.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: bf16 autocast alone (smallest diff, no architecture change), measuring s/epoch, epochs_ran and the composite. A pure speed change that leaves the mechanism identical is the cleanest test of the starvation hypothesis.
ideas.budget_risk: none -- this idea IS about the budget.
ideas.evidence_required: epochs_ran increases materially AND the best epoch stops being the last one (i.e. the run actually converges).
ideas.next_action: ablate the compound change: E10 restores ray_cross_layers=2 at decode 32 (16.6 epochs/h fits the budget).
ideas.implementation: Three non-mechanism changes: (1) I9 dead-tail deletion, output bit-identical. (2) LocalSphericalAttention now gathers neighbours by SLICING a once-padded tensor instead of F.unfold, which materialised (B, C*win*win, H*W) = 1.8 GB for k and v at 64x128 batch 16; verified mathematically identical in forward (rel 1e-7) and backward (input-grad 7e-7). (3) bf16 autocast + TF32 + cudnn.benchmark; metrics are still computed in fp32 so the METRIC never depends on compute precision. Determinism is traded for speed by default (--deterministic True restores it); run-to-run variance is already inside sigma~0.008.
ideas.experiment_split: Split into TWO experiments so throughput and batch size do not confound. E7 = pure speed at E4's exact optimisation (batch 16, lr 3e-4, --epochs 25): isolates 'is RayDPT merely compute-starved?'. E8 = batch tune with the linear lr scaling rule, using the batch that bench_raydpt.py MEASURES as fastest. A bigger batch means fewer optimizer steps per epoch (16->64 is 1800->450), so at a fixed lr it would look like a regression for reasons unrelated to throughput -- a tuning error, not evidence against I8.
ideas.measured_profile: GPU profile, batch 16 bf16, forward: cr64 35.2%, cr32 29.0%, lsa64 15.3%, lsa32 8.1%, refine64 3.7%, enc 3.4%. Cross-attention is 67% of forward. Full train step 278.6 ms = 501 s/epoch; 25 epochs needs 80 ms/step.
ideas.pure_speed_exhausted: bf16 + TF32 + cudnn.benchmark + the ORIGINAL unfold attention gives ~1.4x (713 -> 500 s/epoch) = 7.2 epochs. Both of my LSA rewrites were SLOWER on the real device and were reverted; the CPU forward-time proxy that motivated them was wrong. torch.compile is unavailable here (triton ImportError). Pure speed cannot reach 25 epochs.
ideas.capacity_change: Reaching 25 epochs requires reducing compute, which is a CAPACITY change and is recorded as such. MEASURED: decode_scale 32 (drop cr64+lsa64+refine64) 500 -> 216.5 s/epoch; plus ray_cross_layers 1 -> 147.3 s/epoch at batch 64 = 24.4 epochs/h, 5.00M params, 20.1 GB peak. Ray-conditioning is preserved (per-ray RayBank queries x audio cross-attention, depth decoded per ray direction); only the decode RESOLUTION and the cross-attention DEPTH change.
ideas.justification: I2's oracle licenses the decode cut: a PERFECT predictor at 32x64 scores composite 0.3527 while RayDPT sits at 2.0471. The 64-scale buys resolution the model cannot use, and under a wall-clock budget that compute must be repaid in epochs.
ideas.result: CONFIRMED. E9 (decode 32, 1 cross layer, batch 64, bf16, 25 epochs) fitted 21 epochs at 160.9 s/epoch and CONVERGED: best checkpoint at epoch 18 of 21, val loss flat over the last four. Composite 1.9308 versus the starved E4's 2.0471 -- an improvement of 0.1162, about 14 sigma. RayDPT was compute-starved, exactly as D5 claimed. Note: 21 epochs, not the predicted 24.4, because the throughput bench timed only train steps; validation adds ~14 s/epoch. Bench underestimates.

ideas.origin: reading UNet8Encoder against RayDPT.forward()
ideas.target_bottleneck: 68.7% of RayDPT's parameters are dead
ideas.general_hypothesis: program.md: a gain from deleting code is the best kind. Dead parameters are not free -- they occupy VRAM, they mislead every parameter-count comparison, and they hide the model's real size from the researcher.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: UNet8Encoder builds e1..e8 but RayDPT.forward() only consumes e1..e4. VERIFIED: e5..e8 hold 16.781M of the model's 24.439M parameters (68.7%) and receive NO gradient after a backward pass (p.grad is None). They cost ~67 MB of VRAM and no compute. Deleting them is provably output-equivalent.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: EXACTLY ZERO change to any metric -- that is the point, and it is the test. Model size drops 24.44M -> 7.66M; VRAM falls slightly.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: Any metric changes at all. That would mean e5..e8 were reachable after all, and the deletion is wrong.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: Delete e5..e8; assert the forward output is bit-identical on a fixed input with a fixed seed. No training run needed to verify correctness -- only to confirm the score is unchanged.
ideas.evidence_required: bit-identical forward output.
ideas.next_action: folded into the I8 throughput runs (E7/E8).
ideas.result: VERIFIED bit-identical. Deleted e5..e8 from UNet8Encoder: forward output and D_coarse are exactly equal to the old model given shared weights (max|diff| = 0.0), and 20 parameter tensors disappear. Model 24.44M -> 7.66M params (68.7% removed). Zero metric change expected and zero observed at the forward level.

ideas.origin: E5 improved although its temporal resolution was unchanged -- an unexplained result
ideas.target_bottleneck: the nearest-neighbour resize in _features() turns the time axis into a coarse staircase
ideas.general_hypothesis: An input transform that is a modelling convenience can silently be a defect. If a representation is resized with nearest-neighbour along an axis that carries a physical quantity, the network sees a staircase where the physics is smooth, and may spend capacity on the staircase.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: Nyquist: the win=400 window has an envelope bandwidth ~120 Hz, so hop=160 already samples at 1.2x Nyquist and hop=40 is a 4x OVERSAMPLE -- it adds almost no information. What it does change is the picture: _features resizes (freq, T) to (256,512) with mode='nearest', so T=18 becomes 28.4 px blocks and T=71 becomes 7.2 px blocks. E5's gain may therefore be interpolation smoothness, not temporal information. Test: hold the information EXACTLY at E2's (win 400, hop 160) and switch the resize to bilinear.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: If E5's gain was smoothness, bilinear at hop=160 recovers most of the -0.0227 RMSE gain at zero information cost.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: bilinear at hop=160 gives no RMSE gain -> E5's improvement really did come from the extra time samples, and the nearest resize is not the defect.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: run_base.py --use-log False --feat-interp bilinear (win 400, hop 160). One run. Control = E2.
ideas.budget_risk: none. Identical shapes, identical model, identical frame count.
ideas.evidence_required: RMSE gain comparable to E5's, with information held fixed.
ideas.next_action: deferred confirm: run `--feat-interp bilinear --stft-hop 40` after the RayDPT throughput study.
ideas.strengthened_by: The composite is monotone in staircase block width across E2/E5/E6 (28.4px -> 7.2px -> 2.9px gives 1.8646 -> 1.8514 -> 1.8379), while the two runs' other properties (smear, frequency resolution) move incoherently. Staircase fineness is the only variable that tracks the score across all three.
ideas.result: E8 (win400 hop160 BILINEAR, information EXACTLY equal to control E2): rmse 1.3088 vs 1.3207 (-0.0119), d1 0.5900 vs 0.5938 (-0.0038), composite 1.8658 vs 1.8646 (+0.0012). Removing the staircase recovers 52% of E5's RMSE gain with zero extra information -- the predicted direction -- but it COSTS d1, and the two cancel: the composite does not move. On the composite scale each change is below sigma~0.008 (rmse contributes -0.0074, d1 contributes +0.0083).
ideas.verdict: PARTIAL, NOT CROWNED. The nearest-neighbour staircase is a real contributor to RANGE error -- about half of what E5 gained -- so the resize in _features is a genuine (if small) defect. But it is not the whole story: E5's composite gain (-0.0132, above sigma) survives only with the extra time samples, and smoothing alone buys nothing. Per program.md a near-noise effect requires confirmation; no single run crowns this.
ideas.open_question: Are smoothness and density COMPLEMENTARY? The clean test is bilinear + hop40 versus E5 (nearest + hop40). If the composite improves beyond E5's -0.0132, they add. Cheap, one run -- but it queues behind the RayDPT throughput work, which gates every RayDPT judgement.

ideas.origin: asking what is upstream of BOTH the objective and the representation, after I6 was refuted
ideas.target_bottleneck: batvision reconstructs the whole 256x512 ERP map through a 1x2 bottleneck
ideas.general_hypothesis: A low-pass output can come from a narrow waist in the network rather than from the loss, the input, or the sensor. If every prediction must pass through a handful of numbers, only the coarsest structure survives.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: batvision's unet_256 runs all 8 downs to a 1x2x512 bottleneck: 1024 numbers must reconstruct 131072 pixels. RayDPT has NO such waist -- it decodes per-ray from e4 (16x32) plus cross-attention. If the waist causes the low-pass behaviour, a CONVERGED RayDPT should hold materially more high-frequency azimuthal power than batvision; if it does not, the limit lies further upstream (I7, angular observability).
ideas.expected_metric_effect: a converged RayDPT's azimuthal power at k>=7 should exceed batvision's 19% of GT. Note per I7's caveat this need NOT improve d1.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: A converged RayDPT low-passes just as hard as batvision -> the waist is not the cause, and I7 strengthens.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: Re-run the azimuthal-spectrum diagnostic on RayDPT E7 (25 epochs) versus batvision E3. Zero extra GPU: E7 is already queued for the throughput study.
ideas.budget_risk: none -- rides on an experiment already scheduled.
ideas.evidence_required: the spectrum contrast on a CONVERGED RayDPT.
ideas.preliminary: On the 5-epoch E4 RayDPT holds LESS high-frequency power than batvision (13.7% vs 19.0% of GT), which would argue against the waist -- but E4 never converged, so this is confounded by training and proves nothing. Deferred to E7.
ideas.next_action: none.
ideas.why_dropped: REFUTED, zero GPU. The model WITHOUT the waist is SMOOTHER than the one with it. Compared in the band both can represent (k=7..32): batvision E3 (1x2 bottleneck, converged) holds 0.0290 of azimuthal power; RayDPT E9 (no bottleneck, converged) holds 0.0179; RayDPT E4 (no bottleneck, unconverged) 0.0196. A 1024-number waist is not what makes these predictions low-pass. [FULL-VAL RECHECK: batvision 0.0214 vs RayDPT E9 0.0152 in k=7..32 (3543 samples). Direction confirmed; the original 20-sample figures were unreliable -- see D10.]
ideas.confound_controlled: The naive whole-spectrum comparison would have been invalid: E9 decodes at 32x64, so its azimuthal grid has 64 columns and Nyquist bounds it at k<=32. Its near-zero power at k>32 (0.0007) is a mathematical consequence of the decode grid, not a finding. Only the k=7..32 band, representable by both models, tests the hypothesis.
ideas.scope_of_negative_evidence: Refutes the information-bottleneck explanation for low-pass behaviour on this pair of architectures at this error level. It does not say bottlenecks never matter. It does say that removing the waist -- which RayDPT does by construction -- buys no high-frequency structure, so the limit lies elsewhere (I7: angular observability with two receivers).

ideas.origin: the E9/E10 pair: adding capacity costs convergence, removing it costs ceiling
ideas.target_bottleneck: ray cross-attention costs 67% of forward because it runs over 8192 (and 2048) ray queries
ideas.general_hypothesis: RayDPT is caught in a capacity-versus-convergence trade-off that a plain U-Net is not: at 2 cross layers it cannot converge in the budget, at 1 it converges but is weaker, and both lose to a 54.4M U-Net that converges comfortably. The binding constraint is not how much capacity the ray pathway has, but what each unit of it COSTS. The productive move is to make a cross-attention layer cheaper, not to have fewer of them.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: cr32 attends 2048 ray queries to 2048 e3 tokens; cr64 attends 8192 queries to 512 e4 tokens; together 67% of forward. Two cost levers that preserve per-ray conditioning: (a) shrink the KV set for cr32 from e3 (2048 tokens) to e4 (512), since the coarse audio tokens already carry the global cue and cr16 uses them; (b) shrink the FFN expansion inside CrossBlock from 4x to 2x, which is where most of the per-token FLOPs live. Either keeps every ray query and its cross-attention -- the architectural invariant -- while cutting the price.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: epochs_ran rises toward 25 at 2 layers. If d1 then recovers toward batvision's 0.5949, the angular deficit was a budget artefact all along, and D9 dissolves. If d1 stays flat with a converged 2-layer model, D9 is real and belongs to ray-conditioning itself.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: A cheaper cross layer that converges at 2 layers still leaves d1 near 0.565 -> the deficit is the mechanism's, not the budget's.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: bench (a) and (b) for s/epoch first, zero GPU cost beyond a two-minute benchmark; run only the variant that reaches ~25 epochs at 2 layers.
ideas.evidence_required: a CONVERGED 2-layer RayDPT (best epoch not last), then read d1.
ideas.next_action: bench the two cost levers, then run the one that converges. This is the experiment that finally makes D9 answerable.
ideas.result: Confirmed: the cost of a cross layer is its KV set, not its FFN. ffn 4->2 saves 2 s/epoch; shrinking cr32's KV from e3 (2048 tokens) to e4 (512) takes 231.6 -> 145.4 s/epoch at IDENTICAL parameter count (5.89M). That bought the first converged 2-layer RayDPT (E11), which is what made D9 answerable at all.

ideas.origin: utils/diag_d1.py -- spatial decomposition of D9's deficit on the full val set
ideas.target_bottleneck: far-field range compression: both models predict a GT 8.5 m surface at ~5 m
ideas.general_hypothesis: A loss chooses what a model hedges toward. Masked MAE drives every pixel to the conditional MEDIAN of its posterior; where the far tail is a minority of the mass, the median sits short and the model systematically under-predicts distance. d1 is a RELATIVE threshold, so that short-fall is fatal to it while barely touching RMSE, which far pixels rarely dominate.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: MEASURED (full val): D9's deficit is NOT azimuthal -- flat across azimuth sectors (std 0.0056 on mean 0.0245), ZERO on the floor (el<-30), and exploding with GT depth: at 8-9 m batvision d1 0.4023 vs RayDPT 0.1751. Mean predicted depth at GT 8-9 m: batvision 5.74 m, RayDPT E11 4.97 m. Replacing the dense MAE with a RELATIVE term (|D-gt|/gt) or a symmetric LOG term (|log D - log gt|) charges the same price for a 25% error at 1 m and at 9 m, which is exactly what d1 measures.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: d1 rises, driven by the 7-10 m deciles. RMSE gets slightly WORSE (it rewards hedging toward the mean). ABS_REL improves almost by construction and is therefore NOT evidence.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: the 7-10 m deciles do not improve -> the compression was not caused by the loss, and I13 is dropped whatever the composite does.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: two arms on the RayDPT champion E11's exact architecture: --main-loss rel_mae and --main-loss log_mae. Re-run utils/diag_d1.py on the winner and read the far deciles.
ideas.evidence_required: far-decile d1 improves AND overall d1 improves beyond sigma, with the RMSE cost accounted for.
ideas.next_action: none. Superseded by I14, which attacks the information available to far-field rays rather than the price charged for their errors.
ideas.e13_result: REL_MAE FAILED, and my mechanism reasoning had a sign error. d1 fell -0.0246 (0.5710 -> 0.5464), RMSE rose +0.2352, composite 1.9093 -> 2.0707. ABS_REL improved -0.1117 exactly as predicted and exactly as pre-registered to be NOT evidence.
ideas.sign_error: |D-gt|/gt divides by gt, so a far pixel's error is CHEAPER, not dearer. I wanted to charge far mistakes more and did the opposite. Worse, the term is ASYMMETRIC: under-prediction is bounded (D->0 gives loss 1.0) while over-prediction is unbounded. At gt=1, predicting half costs 0.500 and predicting double costs 1.000. Under uncertainty, calling it short is always cheaper -- the loss ACTIVELY REWARDS the compression it was meant to cure. E13 compressing harder is the mechanism working exactly as (mis)designed.
ideas.what_survives: The DIAGNOSIS stands and is independent of the failed cure: far-field compression is measured, not inferred (GT 8.5 m -> batvision 5.74 m, RayDPT 4.97 m), and it is where d1 is lost. What is refuted is one particular remedy. log_mae is symmetric in the ratio (half and double both cost 0.693), so it carries none of rel_mae's under-prediction incentive; E14 now discriminates 'relative losses are wrong here' from 'rel_mae's asymmetry was wrong'.
ideas.why_dropped: PRE-REGISTERED FALSIFICATION MET. Both relative dense terms made the 7-10 m deciles WORSE, not better. d1 @ (7-8, 8-9, 9-10 m) vs E11: rel_mae (-0.1856, -0.1263, -0.2238), log_mae (-0.1343, -0.0021, -0.1168). Overall d1: E11 0.5710 -> E13 0.5464 -> E14 0.5538. The dense MAE is NOT the cause of far-field compression.
ideas.mechanism_of_failure: Made visible by the predicted-depth table. rel_mae weights each pixel by 1/gt, moving the error budget toward NEAR pixels: at GT 0-1 m it predicts 0.883 m (vs E11's 1.291, GT mid 0.5) and its near-field d1 of 0.4987 beats every model including batvision's 0.2890 -- while its 9-10 m prediction collapses to 3.814 m. My sign error, reproduced exactly in the data. log_mae is symmetric in the ratio and avoids rel_mae's under-prediction incentive, yet still hurts: so it is RELATIVITY itself, not the asymmetry, that is wrong here.
ideas.scope_of_negative_evidence: Refutes the dense term as the cause of far-field compression, on this architecture and budget. The DIAGNOSIS (far-field compression is where d1 is lost) is measured and independent of this failed remedy. It does not refute reweighting in general -- a term that upweights FAR pixels (the opposite of 1/gt) was never tried, and is a live option if I14 fails.

ideas.origin: E9 vs E12, two runs already in the ledger, differing only in which audio tokens the rays attend to
ideas.target_bottleneck: far-field rays cannot see the late, weak echo that carries distance
ideas.general_hypothesis: A ray can only estimate distance from evidence it can attend to. A far surface returns a LATE, WEAK echo, which lives in the late frames of the spectrogram's time axis. Every pooling step of the encoder halves that axis, so coarse audio tokens dilute exactly the evidence far rays need. What matters is not how much the model spends on attention, but WHICH tokens it spends it on.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: MEASURED from runs already collected: E9 (cr32 attends e3, 2048 fine tokens) scores d1 0.2544 at GT 8-9 m; E12 (cr32 attends e4, 512 coarse tokens) scores 0.1579 -- fine tokens buy +0.0965 of far-field d1. batvision, whose U-Net skips carry full time detail into the decoder, scores 0.4023 there. But cr32-on-e3 costs 2048x2048 pairs and never converged (E10). Attention cost is (queries x kv), so route the fine tokens to the CHEAP scale: cr16-on-e3 is 512x2048 = 1.05M pairs, the same price as cr32-on-e4 and 4x cheaper than E10. Parameter count is unchanged (5.89M); measured 169.5 s/epoch = 21.2 epochs.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: the 7-10 m deciles improve over E11; overall d1 rises. RMSE may move either way. ABS_REL is not evidence.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: the 7-10 m deciles do not improve -> drop I14 whatever the composite does, exactly as I13 was dropped.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: E15: --cross-kv16 e3 on E11's architecture, everything else identical. Re-run the far-decile diagnostic.
ideas.budget_risk: low. 24.9 -> 21.2 epochs; still converges. Report epochs_ran and best_epoch.
ideas.evidence_required: far-decile d1 improves AND overall d1 clears sigma.
ideas.next_action: E16 (control) then E15b (treatment), both at lr 6e-4. Pre-registered falsification unchanged: the 7-10m deciles must improve over the CONTROL.
ideas.setback: First attempt (E15) diverged at the parent's lr -- an optimisation failure, not evidence. The coarse head saturated because routing 2048 tokens into cr16 enlarges F16, on which coarse_head+sigmoid sits directly. Re-running at lr 6e-4 with a matched control (E16 = same architecture, same lr, no kv16 change), because lowering the lr alone would confound the treatment against its parent.

ideas.origin: E15's divergence (D11)
ideas.target_bottleneck: RayDPT's coarse head is a sigmoid one 1x1 conv away from the deepest ray-attention output, with no normalisation between
ideas.general_hypothesis: A model whose auxiliary head saturates whenever an upstream path is enlarged is not robust to its own research programme: every capacity change becomes an lr search. Normalising before the head decouples the two.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: m16 = F16 + se4(e4) feeds coarse_head (Conv2d(dim,1,1)) then sigmoid. F16 is a residual accumulation over CrossBlocks; its magnitude grows with the KV set size. Inserting a LayerNorm (or GroupNorm) on m16 before coarse_head should make lc insensitive to F16's scale.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: no metric change at the parent's configuration (it is a reparameterisation), but the kv16=e3 arm should then train at lr 1.2e-3 without diverging.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: the normalised model still diverges with kv16=e3 at 1.2e-3 -> the instability is elsewhere.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: add the norm, verify the parent's composite is unchanged within sigma, then retry kv16=e3 at 1.2e-3.
ideas.evidence_required: parent unchanged AND treatment stable.
ideas.next_action: deferred until I14 is decided at the safe lr; a stability fix must not be bundled with the mechanism it would rescue.
ideas.why_dropped: Predicated on the same refuted premise: normalising before coarse_head cannot fix an instability that persists when coarse_head has no gradient. The --coarse-norm knob stays in the code, off by default, as a documented dead end.

ideas.origin: operator request for a fast experimentation baseline; GPU profile of the E11 champion
ideas.target_bottleneck: iteration speed IS a research variable under a wall-clock budget
ideas.general_hypothesis: Under a fixed wall-clock budget, a cheaper model that converges further scores better AND lets more hypotheses be tested per day. Both effects compound. But a speed change that quietly costs accuracy buys nothing.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: Profiled at the champion's config: cr32 37.1%, lsa32 33.5% of forward. raydpt_win32 5->3 cuts lsa32's neighbourhood from 25 offsets to 9 (1.20x); ffn_mult 4->2 halves the CrossBlock FFN (1.10x more). Together 1.32x: 28.2 epochs/h against 21.3.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: composite within sigma of E11's 1.9093, or better thanks to the extra epochs.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: E17 loses by more than sigma -> the knobs cost real capacity; revert them and keep only the reorganised defaults.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: E17: the new defaults, scored at the full 3600s budget.
ideas.budget_risk: none -- it is the budget that is being bought.
ideas.evidence_required: a SCORED run, not a benchmark. Speed was measured; accuracy must be paid for.
ideas.next_action: Revert the fast knobs from the scored defaults; keep them documented as a screening option.
ideas.e17_result: E17 DIVERGED (best epoch 4 of 25; final val d1 0.0974). It is therefore NOT a test of whether win32=3 + ffn=2 cost accuracy -- it is a second instance of D11. Recorded as `discard`, not `keep`. The speedup (1.32x, measured) stands; its accuracy cost is unmeasured.
ideas.result: PROVISIONALLY SUPPORTED, once the optimiser was fixed. The fast config (win32=3, ffn=2) at lr 3e-4 matches the champion within a tenth of sigma while running 1.21x faster on 10% fewer parameters. But E21 differs from E11 in BOTH the knobs and the lr, so E23 (win5/ffn4 @ 3e-4) is required before the knobs can be called free.
ideas.attribution: E22 (win5/ffn4 @ lr 6e-4) = 1.9125 fills the 2x2. At MATCHED lr the fast knobs cost +0.0051 (E22 -> E20), below sigma. At MATCHED architecture, dropping lr from 1.2e-3 to 6e-4 costs +0.0032 (E11 -> E22), also below sigma. Neither effect is individually measurable, and E21 (both changes, lr 3e-4) lands at 1.9099, +0.0007 from the champion. So the fast defaults are accuracy-neutral within noise while running 1.21x faster on 10% fewer parameters. E23 (win5/ffn4 @ 3e-4) completes the square.
ideas.why_dropped: REFUTED once the 2x2 was CLOSED. E23 (win5/ffn4 @ lr 3e-4) scores 1.8962 -- the best RayDPT of the project. At the lr we actually adopt, the fast knobs cost +0.0137 (E23 -> E21), ABOVE sigma. They are not free.
ideas.why_i_nearly_missed_it: E21's parity with E11 (+0.0006) was a COINCIDENCE of two opposing real effects: lr 3e-4 GAINS 0.0131 over 1.2e-3 at fixed architecture, and the knobs LOSE 0.0137 at fixed lr. Both clear sigma; they nearly cancel. At lr 6e-4 the knob cost is only +0.0051 (sub-sigma), which is what made them look free. Had I stopped before E23 I would have adopted a config that quietly costs 0.0137 -- and I would have cited two sub-sigma cells as evidence.
ideas.what_survives: The knobs remain a legitimate SCREENING configuration: 1.21x faster (118.8 vs 143.3 s/epoch) on 10% fewer parameters for a known, measured price of 0.0137 composite. That is a trade, now quantified, not a free lunch. Scored runs use win5/ffn4.

ideas.origin: E15 and E17 diverging identically at the parent's lr (D11)
ideas.target_bottleneck: RayDPT's coarse-layout auxiliary is its only failure point, and it was already measured inert
ideas.general_hypothesis: A component that contributes nothing and can destroy training is worse than no component. Deleting it is not a tuning convenience; it removes the coupling that turns every architecture change into an lr search.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: lc = masked_mae(sigmoid(coarse_head(m16)), gt_c, m_c). I6 measured BOTH auxiliaries inert on batvision: zeroing them moved the composite by +0.0046 (below sigma) after removing 58.2% of the gradient. On RayDPT the same term is the sole locus of divergence in E15 and E17. Setting w_coarse_layout=0 removes the sigmoid that saturates and the dead gradient path behind it.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: composite unchanged within sigma, and the fast architecture trains without diverging at lr 1.2e-3.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: the composite degrades by more than sigma -> lc is load-bearing for RayDPT even though it was inert for batvision. That is a finding about ray-conditioned decoders, not a defect; E19 (small weight) or I15 (normalise before the head) would then be the answer.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: E18: fast defaults + --w-coarse-layout 0, scored at the full budget.
ideas.evidence_required: stable training AND composite within sigma of E11's 1.9093.
ideas.next_action: none. Superseded by I18 (the optimiser).
ideas.why_dropped: PREMISE REFUTED BY ITS OWN EXPERIMENT. I17 held that lc was RayDPT's failure point. E18 removed lc from the loss entirely (w_coarse_layout=0) and the run still destabilised: mae x1.51 at epoch 7 with lc contributing zero gradient. lc is a symptom, not a cause.
ideas.what_survives: The measurement that coarse_head is a pure side-branch stands (verified: --coarse-norm changes D_coarse and leaves D bit-identical), and I6's finding that the auxiliaries are inert stands. Deleting lc is still a legitimate simplification -- it just does not buy stability, which is what I claimed it would.
ideas.e18_full_trajectory: E18 never recovered. mae 0.0632 -> 0.0953 (ep7) -> 0.1270 (ep12); val d1 0.5342 -> 0.1307. lc plateaued at exactly 0.1849 for four epochs -- D_coarse fully saturated, emitting a constant. Best checkpoint at epoch 6, composite 2.0424. Recorded `discard`.

ideas.origin: E18 destabilising with the auxiliary removed -- an intervention, not a correlation
ideas.target_bottleneck: lr 1.2e-3 (linear-scaled for batch 64) is at the edge of stability for RayDPT, and any capacity change tips it
ideas.general_hypothesis: The linear scaling rule is a heuristic for convnets trained with SGD; it is not a law, and for a residual attention stack it can land exactly on the edge of divergence. A model whose lr must be re-tuned for every architectural knob cannot be researched quickly, whatever its FLOPs.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: lr = 3e-4 x (64/16) = 1.2e-3. E9, E11 and E12 survived it; E15 (larger KV) and E17 (win32=3, ffn=2) did not, and E18 shows the failure is in the trunk, not the auxiliary. Gradient clipping is already 1.0. Sweep lr at the fast config -- the mechanism is untouched, so this is a `tune`, not a `new`.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: a stable run whose composite is within sigma of E11's, at a lr low enough that the architecture knobs no longer matter for stability.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: the fast config destabilises at every lr that still converges inside the budget -> the instability is architectural after all, and the win32/ffn knobs must be reverted.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: three scored runs at lr 1.2e-3 (known), 6e-4, 3e-4 on the fast config. Report best_epoch, epochs_ran, and whether mae ever jumps by more than 1.2x between epochs.
ideas.budget_risk: none per run; it costs GPU hours, which is precisely what the fast config is meant to buy back.
ideas.evidence_required: a stable, converged fast config; then re-test the win32/ffn accuracy question that E17 could not answer.
ideas.next_action: E22 (win5/ffn4 @ 6e-4) and E23 (win5/ffn4 @ 3e-4) complete the 2x2. E23 vs E21 is the clean answer to whether the fast knobs cost accuracy at the lr we will adopt.
ideas.e19_skipped: E19 (w_coarse_layout=0.1) was queued as a second stability point for I17. Skipped: E18 refuted I17's premise, so E19 would have spent a GPU hour testing a dropped idea.
ideas.result: CONFIRMED. At lr 6e-4 the fast config is perfectly stable: E20's mae never ROSE between epochs (max ratio 0.99, against E17's 1.46 and E18's 1.51 at lr 1.2e-3), and it converged -- best checkpoint epoch 23 of 26. The instability was the OPTIMISER. Neither the architecture nor the coarse-layout auxiliary caused it, and E18 proved the latter by removing it and diverging anyway.
ideas.caveat: E20's composite of 1.9176 is +0.0083 against E11's 1.9093 -- just over the sigma of 0.008 -- but E20 differs from E11 in TWO ways (the knobs AND the lr), so the gap cannot be attributed. E22 (E11's architecture at lr 6e-4) fills the 2x2.
ideas.ladder: Monotone, and it kills the linear scaling rule for this model. fast config @ 1.2e-3 DIVERGED (E17); @ 6e-4 stable, 1.9176 (E20); @ 3e-4 stable, 1.9099 (E21). 3e-4 is the ORIGINAL batch-16 lr -- so scaling lr with batch was never needed, and doing it put every run on the edge of divergence.
ideas.practical_result: E21 matches the E11 champion (1.9093) to +0.0007 -- one tenth of sigma -- with 10% fewer parameters (5.29M vs 5.89M) and 118.8 vs 143.3 s/epoch, a 1.21x speedup. Its rmse is actually better (1.3231 vs 1.3276); d1 is identical (0.5706 vs 0.5710).
ideas.e22_confirms: E22 is stable too (max mae jump 0.99). Across the whole 2x2 the ONLY diverging cell is lr 1.2e-3 with a changed architecture. The optimiser, not the architecture.
ideas.final: lr 3e-4 is not merely stable, it is BETTER: at win5/ffn4 it beats lr 1.2e-3 by 0.0131 (E23 1.8962 vs E11 1.9093), above sigma. The linear scaling rule did not just risk divergence -- it cost accuracy in every run that survived it. New RayDPT champion: E23.

ideas.origin: the measured far-field failure (D9 reframed) + the physical meaning of the encoder's width axis
ideas.target_bottleneck: the model must LEARN that echo delay encodes depth, and it fails to, collapsing far surfaces toward the median
ideas.general_hypothesis: Depth from echoes is a time-of-flight measurement: a surface at depth d returns its echo at t = 2d/c. A network that regresses a scalar depth must discover that correspondence from data. A network whose ARCHITECTURE encodes it does not. Bake the physics into where the model looks, instead of paying for it in capacity and in a loss that hedges toward the median.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: The encoder's WIDTH axis is time -- spec is (freq 256, time 512), so e3 is (freq 32, time 64) -- and its 64 columns map one-to-one onto depth hypotheses spanning 0.08-9.92 m. EchoDelayVolume: for each ray r and hypothesis j, the ray query attends over FREQUENCY within time column j alone (azimuth lives in the per-frequency ILD/IPD; time must not be mixed across hypotheses); a small MLP scores each hypothesis; a softmax over the DEPTH axis gives p(d|ray); depth_r = sum_j p_j d_j, a soft-argmax over echo delay. +34k parameters.
ideas.why_it_attacks_the_measured_failure: (a) Far-field range compression: at GT 8-9 m RayDPT predicts 4.97 m and scores d1 0.1751 against batvision's 0.4023. A distribution over depth bins has no conditional-median collapse, and the evidence for a far surface sits in a LATE column that the structure now points at directly. E9-vs-E12 already showed fine time-resolved tokens buy +0.0965 of far-field d1. (b) It replaces coarse_head -- a sigmoid one 1x1 conv from the deepest attention output, which saturated and diverged (D11) -- with a softmax over physically meaningful bins.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: the 7-10 m deciles improve; overall d1 rises; RMSE improves. If only ABS_REL moves, the mechanism is unsupported.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: the 7-10 m deciles do not improve over E21 -> DROP, whatever the composite does. Exactly the test that killed I13.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: E24: the current defaults + --depth-volume True. E21 (1.9099) is the control -- the defaults ARE its config, so the run differs in one thing.
ideas.budget_risk: low; +34k params, benchmarked before the run.
ideas.evidence_required: far-decile d1 improves AND overall d1 clears sigma.
ideas.next_action: Do NOT crown. Test the ONE compatible combination the scope predicts: EchoDelayVolume + fine-time-token routing (I14, kv=e3), which supplies the same far-field signal through a different path -- and which needs its own lr, since kv=e3 diverged before (D11).
ideas.result: PASSED its pre-registered falsification -- the same test that killed I13. Against the matched control E21, EVERY far decile improved: 7-8 m +0.0692, 8-9 m +0.0759, 9-10 m +0.0595 (and 6-7 m +0.0434). The mean predicted depth at GT 8-9 m rose 4.758 m -> 5.238 m (batvision 5.738 m), so the range compression the mechanism was designed to attack actually loosened. Overall d1 +0.0027, rmse -0.0048, composite 1.9099 -> 1.8987 (-0.0112, above sigma). Stable (max mae jump 0.99), converged (best epoch 23 of 24). Costs 12 s/epoch and LESS VRAM (11.8 vs 13.8 GB).
ideas.significance: This is the first mechanism in the project whose PRE-REGISTERED prediction was confirmed. I1's metric prediction inverted, I2/I4/I6/I11 were refuted, I13 was killed by this same far-decile test, I17 was refuted by its own experiment. Confirmation here means the physical story -- an echo from depth d arrives at t=2d/c, and the encoder's width axis IS that delay -- is doing the work, not a lucky hyperparameter.
ideas.caveat: E24 carried the fast knobs (win32=3, ffn=2), which E23 measured at +0.0137. The mechanism has never been run on the champion architecture, and it has ONE draw. program.md: never crown a candidate on a single draw.
ideas.e25_inconclusive: E25 (champion architecture + EchoDelayVolume) scored 1.9083 against E23's 1.8962 -- the OPPOSITE sign from E24, which beat its control by 0.0112. But E25 did NOT converge: best checkpoint = LAST epoch (21/21). And it was triply disadvantaged, all in the same direction and none of it the mechanism: EchoDelayVolume costs 12 s/epoch, so on the champion architecture (157.1 vs 144.6) it fits one epoch fewer; --epochs 26 meant its cosine was cut off at 81% of schedule (E23 reached 92%); and its best checkpoint is the last. This is the E10 trap again -- reading a metric off a model that was still learning.
ideas.correction: Re-run with --epochs matched to what fits (21), so cosine anneals to zero inside the budget (idea I3). Two draws: E27 and E28.
ideas.verdict: A REAL mechanism with a SCOPED effect, not a champion. On a capacity-starved parent (fast: win3, ffn2) it improves every far decile and beats its control by 0.0112 (E24 vs E21). On the champion parent (win5, ffn4) the far deciles REGRESS and it loses by 0.0309 (E27 vs E23). The champion's wider LSA and larger FFN already buy the far-field accuracy EchoDelayVolume targets, so the two compete for capacity instead of composing.
ideas.scope: EchoDelayVolume helps when the decoder is starved of far-field capacity; it hinders when that capacity is already present. It is a specialist, not a global champion -- program.md: a method can FAIL to become champion yet PASS as a hypothesis if it has a clear, reproducible, scoped effect. This qualifies.

ideas.origin: DC2 -- extend the one confirmed mechanism at finer time resolution
ideas.target_bottleneck: EchoDelayVolume reads e3 (time 64), which is the STFT's 512 columns pooled 8x -- its delay resolution may be the limit
ideas.general_hypothesis: If the echo-delay cost volume works because it aligns depth with echo arrival time, then its accuracy should scale with how finely it resolves that time. e3 has 64 time columns; e2 has 128. Doubling the delay resolution should sharpen the far field, where echoes are late and closely spaced.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: EchoDelayVolume on e2 (freq 64, time 128) instead of e3 (freq 32, time 64). Each time column is a depth hypothesis d=c*t/2; e2 spans 0.04-9.96m in 128 bins vs e3's 0.08-9.92m in 64. On the FAST parent where I19 helps (control E24 1.8987).
ideas.expected_metric_effect: the 7-10m deciles improve FURTHER than E24's; far-field d1 rises.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: the far deciles do not improve over E24 -> delay resolution was not the limit, drop.
ideas.budget_risk: low; ~free params. e2 has 2x the columns so the volume's attention is 2x -- benchmark.
ideas.next_action: E29 running.
ideas.composite: E29 composite 1.9006 vs E24 1.8987, delta +0.0019 (below sigma). Converged (best 14/22). The far-decile verdict -- the actual pre-registered test -- is pending: the diagnostic correctly blocked on the eval_lock while E30 trains, rather than stealing the GPU.
ideas.note: e2 doubles the volume's time columns (128 vs 64) but costs ~0 params; s/epoch 153 vs E24's 130 (the fast parent), still converges. Whether the finer delay resolution actually moves the far field is the open question.
ideas.why_dropped: PRE-REGISTERED FALSIFICATION FAILED. Reading e2 (time 128) instead of e3 (time 64) did NOT improve the far deciles over E24 -- all three regressed: 7-8m -0.0427, 8-9m -0.0442, 9-10m -0.0154. Doubling the volume's delay resolution did not sharpen the far field. Composite was neutral (+0.0019) but the composite is not the test.
ideas.interpretation: The volume's LIMIT is not its time resolution. e3's 64 columns already resolve 0-10m at 0.16m spacing, finer than the achieved RMSE (1.3m). More columns cannot help a model whose far-field error is ~1.3m, not 0.16m. The through-line ('finer time = better far field') held ACROSS very different resolutions (18->71 STFT frames, e4->e3 tokens) but SATURATES within the volume itself.
ideas.scope_of_negative_evidence: Refutes finer TIME BINNING of the echo-delay volume, on the fast parent. It does not refute the volume itself (I19 stands, scoped) nor coarser-grained time-resolution levers upstream.

ideas.origin: the scope of I19 (E27): EchoDelayVolume competes with dense capacity, so pair it with a DIFFERENT far-field path
ideas.target_bottleneck: combine two non-overlapping far-field mechanisms
ideas.general_hypothesis: I19's scope (D12) is that it helps a decoder STARVED of far-field signal and hinders one that already has it via dense capacity. Fine-token routing (I14, cross_kv32=e3) supplies far-field signal through a different path -- the 2048 fine time tokens into cr32's cross-attention. Two starvation cures on different paths should compose where a starvation cure plus dense capacity did not.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: EchoDelayVolume + cross_kv32=e3 on the fast parent. kv=e3 gives cr32 fine time tokens; the volume gives the coarse rays a soft-argmax over delay. Control E24 (volume only, kv=e4).
ideas.expected_metric_effect: far deciles improve beyond E24; the two effects at least partly add.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: no improvement over E24, OR divergence (kv=e3 fell over at lr 1.2e-3, D11; must be stable at 3e-4).
ideas.budget_risk: medium; kv=e3 is 4x cr32 attention. E10 showed it fits at ~15 epochs at 2 layers, but the fast config is cheaper.
ideas.next_action: E30 running; watch for the D11 divergence signature (lc/mae spike).
ideas.e30_stability: STABLE at lr 3e-4: E30 (EDE + cross_kv32=e3) trained normally through epoch 2 (mae 0.1010 -> 0.0679), no D11 spike. First confirmation that I18's lr fix makes kv=e3 -- which diverged at lr 1.2e-3 -- trainable. 217 s/epoch.
ideas.why_dropped: PRE-REGISTERED FALSIFICATION FAILED, and it is confounded on top. E30 (EchoDelayVolume + kv=e3) regressed every far decile vs E24 (7-8m -0.0416, 8-9m -0.0243, 9-10m -0.0349) and its composite is 1.9508, far worse. It was ALSO budget-starved: kv=e3 is 4x cr32 attention (218.5 s/epoch), so only 16 epochs fit vs E24's 24.
ideas.interpretation: The two far-field paths do NOT compose -- they behave like E27's EDE+dense: redundant, not complementary. Both the volume and kv=e3 target the SAME signal (fine echo timing), so stacking them adds cost without adding information, exactly as D12 warned when the paths overlap. The scope prediction was wrong: 'different path' is not enough; the paths must carry DIFFERENT information, and these carry the same.
ideas.what_survives: E30 confirmed kv=e3 is STABLE at lr 3e-4 (I18's fix), which reopens kv=e3 as a research knob that D11 had closed. That is a real infrastructure gain even though the combine failed.

ideas.origin: D13 -- the decoder-side far-field lever saturated; move the question upstream
ideas.target_bottleneck: the ENCODER pools the STFT's 512 time columns down to e3's 64 (8x) before EchoDelayVolume ever reads them
ideas.general_hypothesis: No downstream structure can recover time resolution the encoder has already averaged away. If the far-field limit is encoder pooling, an echo-delay volume that reads the STFT directly -- 512 time columns, 2 cm depth spacing, encoder bypassed -- should improve the far field where e3 (64 cols) and e2 (128) saturated. If it also saturates, the limit is not time resolution but the sensor (I7).
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: EchoDelayVolume with depth_volume_src=raw reads the (in_ch, 256, 512) spec tensor. Each of its 512 time columns is a depth hypothesis at 2 cm spacing. This is the cleanest possible test of D13's two branches -- it removes ALL encoder pooling from the volume's input, not just 2x.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: the 7-10m deciles improve over E24; if they do, encoder pooling was the bottleneck.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: the far deciles saturate again (no improvement over E24) -> time resolution is NOT the far-field limit; it is the sensor. This CLOSES the whole time-resolution line and reopens I7 / the representation.
ideas.budget_risk: low-medium; raw has 512 columns (8x e3's attention) but only in_ch=5 channels, so cheaper per column. Benchmarked implicitly by the run.
ideas.evidence_required: far-decile improvement over E24, on the fast parent.
ideas.next_action: E31 running. This run DECIDES between encoder-limited and sensor-limited far field.
ideas.implementation_note: First E31 OOMed: raw's (B,N=512,F=256,T=512) logits are 9GB at batch 64 (the CPU smoke at batch 2 hid it). Fixed with freq_stride=8 in EchoDelayVolume's input conv, pooling F 256->32 -- the SAME frequency size as e3, so the run now isolates purely the TIME axis (512 vs e3's 64). Frequency is only ever reduced by the volume's own attention softmax, so pre-pooling it loses nothing. Logits now 1.1GB; peak 25GB at batch 64, verified with a batch-64 GPU smoke (the CPU smoke that missed the OOM is why).
ideas.cleaner_than_planned: This is a BETTER test than intended: raw now differs from e3 ONLY in time resolution (512 vs 64), not frequency. A pure encoder-time-pooling probe.
ideas.why_dropped: PRE-REGISTERED FALSIFICATION FAILED, decisively. Bypassing the encoder's 8x time pooling entirely -- EchoDelayVolume reading the STFT's 512 time columns at 2cm spacing, frequency matched to e3 so ONLY time differs -- made the far deciles WORSE, not better: 7-8m -0.0489, 8-9m -0.0412, 9-10m -0.0421 vs E24. Mean predicted depth at GT 8-9m FELL from 5.238m to 5.092m. The encoder's time pooling was NOT the bottleneck.
ideas.closes_the_line: This is the decider D13 asked for, and it settles branch B. Across FIVE runs -- E24 (bare, helps), E27 (dense), E29 (finer bins), E30 (fine tokens), E31 (raw, encoder bypassed) -- the first dose of echo-delay time structure helps and every subsequent increase in time resolution, by any path, does nothing or hurts. Far-field d1 is NOT limited by time resolution anywhere in the pipeline. The remaining candidate is the SENSOR: two microphones under-determine range/angle for far, faint echoes (I7).

ideas.origin: user request to open the representation lever after D13 (sensor-limited far field)
ideas.target_bottleneck: more range information from two microphones (coherence / late-tail energy)
ideas.general_hypothesis: If the far field is sensor-limited, the only remaining far-field lever is a representation that extracts more range information the current 5ch stack discards.
ideas.why_dropped: REFUTED at zero GPU by two waveform-level correlation probes. (1) Magnitude-squared coherence -- which should fall for distant, reverberant scenes -- correlates only +0.17 with mean depth and the WRONG sign with far-surface fraction. (2) Late-tail waveform energy (round-trip 14-20m, where far echoes arrive) correlates only -0.30 with far-surface fraction, again the wrong sign. The range signal for distant surfaces is not strongly present in the two-mic waveform in a form a representation could surface. This confirms D13's sensor-limit at the representation level, not just the architecture level.
ideas.scope_of_negative_evidence: Refutes coherence and late-tail energy as far-field cues on this dataset. It does not test multi-resolution STFT, which combines windows rather than re-deriving a single statistic -- but see I24 for why that is deprioritised.
ideas.next_action: none. The far field is sensor-limited at both the architecture (D13) and representation levels.

ideas.origin: pixel-weighted decomposition of the d1 gap -- the far field is 1% of pixels
ideas.target_bottleneck: the 1-2 m near field, which is 52.5% of pixels and over half the total d1 gap to batvision
ideas.general_hypothesis: I spent five studies on far-field range compression because its per-pixel gap is dramatic (+0.12 to +0.17). But the far field (7-10m) is only 5% of pixels; weighted by pixel count it contributes +0.0070 of the d1 gap. The 1-6m field is 77% of pixels and +0.0172 -- and 1-2m ALONE is 52.5% of pixels and where RayDPT trails batvision by +0.0195, over half the total. The productive lever is the NEAR field, not the far.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: At 1-2m RayDPT scores d1 0.7506 vs batvision 0.7700. These are the room walls -- strong, early, well-localised echoes. The question is why a ray-conditioned model with per-direction queries loses to a plain U-Net exactly where the signal is strongest. Candidates: the x8 decode upsample blurs near-field boundaries (I2 was refuted for the far field but near-field edges are different); or the DPT skips' pixel-index correspondence (I5) hurts most where structure is sharp.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: any near-field mechanism should move the 1-2m decile, which dominates the pixel-weighted metric.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: the 1-2m decile does not respond to near-field-targeted changes -> the near-field gap is also sensor/data-limited.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: first a diagnostic: WHERE in 1-2m does RayDPT lose -- edges (boundary pixels) or interiors? Compare d1 on boundary vs interior masks. Zero GPU on existing checkpoints.
ideas.evidence_required: a spatial characterisation of the 1-2m loss before any architecture change.
ideas.next_action: A near-field compression cure that is NOT time-resolution: candidates are (a) a small per-image scale correction learned from the coarse layout, (b) the log-depth output parameterisation (predict log-depth so equal RATIO errors are penalised equally, matching d1), (c) revisiting the loss now that the diagnosis is metric-shaped rather than range-shaped.
ideas.diagnosis:
FULL-VAL spatial characterisation of the 1-2m near-field gap (52.5% of pixels):
  (1) BOUNDARY vs INTERIOR: the gap is entirely INTERIOR. On depth discontinuities RayDPT ties batvision (0.4447 vs 0.4471); on flat wall interiors it trails by +0.0196. So it is NOT the x8 decode upsample blurring edges -- it is absolute depth on smooth surfaces.
  (2) BIAS vs VARIANCE: RayDPT's interior bias (+0.132m) and std (0.718m) are both SMALLER than batvision's (+0.175m, 0.762m). Better mean, worse d1.
  (3) RATIO DISTRIBUTION resolves the paradox: d1 is a +-25% threshold. RayDPT piles 32% of pixels at pred/gt 0.9-1.0 (slightly under) where batvision piles 40% at 1.0-1.11 (dead centre). RayDPT also has a thicker under-prediction tail (<0.8: 9.8% vs 7.8%). Same compression as the far field -- masked-MAE pulls walls slightly NEAR -- but the near field is half the pixels.
  (4) EchoDelayVolume does NOT fix it: on 1-2m interiors E24 vs E21 is -0.0031. The volume is far-field-only; near-field compression needs a different mechanism.
ideas.reframing: The champion's real deficit is near-field range compression on flat walls, a metric-shaped effect (median-pull just under the +-25% band), unreached by the entire echo-delay line. It is the same disease as the far field but in the 50%-of-pixels band and NOT curable by the same tool.

ideas.origin: I24 diagnosis -- near-field median-pull is metric-shaped, not range-shaped
ideas.target_bottleneck: masked-MAE on normalised depth optimises the ARITHMETIC median, which sits below the geometric median (pred/gt ratio 1) that d1 rewards
ideas.general_hypothesis: d1 is a +-25% RATIO threshold. A model that minimises absolute error on linear depth converges to the conditional arithmetic median, which for a right-skewed depth posterior is pulled BELOW the geometric median -- so predictions land at ratio 0.9-1.0, just under the band centre, exactly the near-field pattern I24 measured. Regressing in log-depth makes the optimum the geometric median (ratio 1), aligning the model's fixed point with the metric.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: Output D = exp(log d_min + sigmoid(head) * log(1/d_min)) instead of sigmoid(head). Same parameters, same masked-MAE code; only the map from the head's logit to depth changes, from linear to geometric. The model's free variable is now log-depth.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: 1-2m INTERIOR d1 rises toward batvision's 0.7719; overall d1 rises. If only ABS_REL moves, or the interior does not improve, it failed.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: 1-2m interior d1 does not improve over E23 -> drop, whatever the composite does.
ideas.distinct_from_I13: I13 re-WEIGHTED the loss (|D-gt|/gt) on a RANGE-shaped mis-diagnosis and failed. I25 re-PARAMETERISES the output on a correct METRIC-shaped diagnosis (median-pull). Different intervention, different diagnosis.
ideas.evidence_required: near-field interior d1 improvement.
ideas.next_action: E32 running; control E23.
ideas.composite: E32 composite 1.9102 vs E23 1.8962, +0.0140 (worse), overall d1 -0.0044. Converged (best 15/22). But composite/overall-d1 are NOT the test -- the pre-registered locus is 1-2m interior d1, pending behind E33.
ideas.caveat_to_check: A subtlety in the implementation: the model regresses log-depth, but masked-MAE is still applied to the LINEAR out['D']. So the model's free variable is log d, yet the LOSS is |D_lin - gt_lin|. Whether that alone moves the optimum from arithmetic to geometric median depends on how the sigmoid+exp curvature interacts with the linear MAE -- it is not guaranteed by construction the way it would be if the loss itself were in log space. The near-field diagnostic is the real test; if it fails, the cleaner form is masked-MAE ON log-depth (|log D - log gt|), which is unambiguously the geometric-median objective.
ideas.why_dropped: PRE-REGISTERED FALSIFICATION FAILED. 1-2m interior d1 did not move: E23 0.7523 -> E32 0.7521. The pred/gt ratio histogram is UNCHANGED -- still 32% piled at 0.9-1.0 (just under) vs batvision's 40% at 1.0-1.11 (centre). Re-parameterising the OUTPUT to log-depth while keeping masked-MAE on LINEAR depth does NOT move the optimum to the geometric median, exactly the loss-space subtlety flagged before the run: the free variable is log d but the loss is |D_lin - gt_lin|, so its optimum is still the arithmetic median.
ideas.correct_form: The unambiguous fix is masked-MAE computed ON log-depth (|log D - log gt|), which IS the geometric-median objective by definition. That is main_loss=log_mae, already implemented (from I13). I25's mechanism survives; only its IMPLEMENTATION (output re-param) was wrong. Re-test as log_mae LOSS on the linear champion.
ideas.distinct_from_I13_note: I13 tested log_mae as a loss but on the FAST parent and judged on far deciles (range mis-diagnosis). Here it is judged on the near-field interior (correct diagnosis) on the champion. Same knob, different hypothesis and locus -- a legitimate re-test, not a repeat.

ideas.origin: two independent diagnoses cure two non-overlapping deficits
ideas.target_bottleneck: combine near-field median-pull cure (log-out) with far-field range cure (EchoDelayVolume)
ideas.general_hypothesis: E30 failed to combine two far-field cures because they carried the SAME information. log-out (near) and EchoDelayVolume (far) target DIFFERENT, non-overlapping deficits, so they should compose. This is the combine the two diagnoses jointly predict.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: --depth-out log --depth-volume True on the champion. log-out fixes the 52.5%-of-pixels near field; EchoDelayVolume fixes the 5%-of-pixels far field. Control E23; also compare vs E32 (log alone) to confirm additivity.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: both the 1-2m interior AND the 7-10m deciles improve; the composite gains from both.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: no improvement over the better of E32 / E24-transplanted -> the two do not compose after all.
ideas.evidence_required: joint near+far improvement.
ideas.next_action: E33 running.
ideas.why_dropped: E33 (log-out + EDE) 1-2m interior d1 0.7488 -- WORSE than E23, and its far deciles (7-8m 0.3069, 8-9m 0.2008) beat E32 but not E23's linear 8-9m 0.2306. With log-out itself refuted (I25), the combine has no near-field cure to contribute, and EDE on the champion loses as E27 already showed. Composite 1.8990, neutral vs E23.
ideas.what_survives: Confirms once more that EchoDelayVolume on the champion (win5/ffn4) does not help the far field (E27's parent-dependence), independent of the output parameterisation.

ideas.origin: I25's correct implementation -- put the loss, not the output, in log space
ideas.target_bottleneck: near-field median-pull: the loss must optimise the geometric median, which requires the LOSS in log space, not the output
ideas.general_hypothesis: d1 is a ratio threshold, so its matching loss charges equal RATIO errors equally -- that is |log D - log gt|, whose optimum is the conditional geometric median (ratio 1). E32 showed re-parameterising the OUTPUT is insufficient; the LOSS itself must be in log space.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: main_loss=log_mae on the champion (linear output). Same masked structure, error measured as |log D - log gt|. Judge on 1-2m interior d1 and its ratio histogram: the 0.9-1.0 pile should shift toward 1.0-1.11.
