ideas.expected_metric_effect: 1-2m interior d1 rises toward batvision's; the ratio histogram centres.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: the ratio histogram does not shift -> the near-field pull is not loss-shaped and I27 (and the whole objective line) is done.
ideas.distinct_from_I13: I13 ran log_mae on the FAST parent judged on FAR deciles (range mis-diagnosis, converged, failed there). I27 runs it on the CHAMPION judged on the NEAR-field interior (correct median-pull diagnosis). Different parent, different locus, different hypothesis.
ideas.evidence_required: near-field ratio histogram shift.
ideas.next_action: Either a Huber-log loss (centre without loosening the tail), or accept that the near-field pull is a small, partially-loss-shaped effect and the champion is near a metric-shaped ceiling. Decide at a synthesize checkpoint.
ideas.result: HALF-CONFIRMED, and instructive. log_mae LOSS on the champion moved the 1-2m ratio histogram exactly as predicted: the pile at 0.9-1.0 fell 32.1%->29.1% and the centre 1.0-1.11 rose 29.2%->32.1% (+2.9%, toward batvision's 40.4%). So the median-pull mechanism is REAL and log-space loss IS its lever -- the diagnosis and the direction both hold. BUT 1-2m interior d1 fell -0.0034, because the same loss thickened the OVER-prediction tail (>1.25: 15.0%->16.2%). Centring the bulk cost accuracy in the tail; the two cancel at the threshold.
ideas.interpretation: log_mae penalises equal ratio errors equally, so relative to MAE it relaxes the penalty on large LINEAR errors -- which at 1-2m are the over-predictions. It fixes the systematic under-pull but loosens the tail. The net on a +-25% threshold is a wash. A pure geometric-median loss is not enough; the near-field needs the centre AND the tail, which points at a HUBER-like log loss (quadratic near ratio 1, linear in the tail) or a small d1-surrogate term, not a plain log_mae.
ideas.scope_of_negative_evidence: Refutes plain log_mae as a near-field d1 cure on the champion. Confirms median-pull is real and log-space is the right axis. The refined form (bounded/Huber log loss) is untested.

ideas.origin: E34's half-confirmation -- log_mae centres the histogram but loosens the tail
ideas.target_bottleneck: a near-field loss that centres the ratio bulk at 1 WITHOUT relaxing the over-prediction tail
ideas.general_hypothesis: E34 proved median-pull is real and log-space is its axis, but plain log_mae trades centre for tail: quadratic-like near 0 it would centre harder, but log_mae is LINEAR everywhere, so its only lever on the bulk is the same slope it applies to the tail. A Huber on the log-ratio decouples them -- quadratic inside d1's band centres the bulk, linear outside keeps the tail penalty.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: log_huber: Huber(r) with r=log D - log gt and delta=log(1.25)=0.223, the exact +-25% threshold. |r|<=delta -> 0.5 r^2/delta (quadratic, steep pull to ratio 1); |r|>delta -> |r|-0.5*delta (linear, same asymptotic slope as log_mae). Verified numerically: at ratio 0.9 the loss is 0.025 vs log_mae's 0.105 (4x steeper centring), while at ratio 2.0 it matches log_mae's slope.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: 1-2m interior d1 improves over BOTH E23 (mae) and E34 (log_mae); the ratio histogram centres AND the >1.25 tail does not grow past E23's 15.0%.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: the tail still grows past 15.0%, OR interior d1 does not beat E23 -> the near-field pull is not cleanly loss-shapeable; close the objective line for the near field.
ideas.evidence_required: interior d1 up AND tail contained.
ideas.next_action: E35 running; controls E23 and E34.

discrepancies.observation: log1p magnitude compression is a no-op at 2ch: E1 (log) composite 1.8784 vs E0 (nolog) 1.8854, delta 0.0070 < sigma 0.008. The two runs SPLIT the underlying metrics -- log wins rmse (1.3116 vs 1.3186) and d1 (0.5808 vs 0.5785), nolog wins abs_rel (0.4143 vs 0.4211).
discrepancies.why_it_matters: The pre-registered detailed hypothesis predicted log would help MORE at 2ch than at 5ch, because in the 5ch stack ILD already supplies a log-domain ratio. At 2ch it does not help at all, so that half of the prediction is unsupported. A metric split with a sub-sigma delta is the signature of noise, not of a mechanism.
discrepancies.diagnostic_value: If E2/E3 show log ALSO does nothing at 5ch, then dynamic-range compression is simply irrelevant to this task -- which would point at time-of-flight (I1), not level statistics, as the operative cue. That is a cheap, strong inference from data already being collected.
discrepancies.next_action: none. A third draw would crown the log effect as an independent finding; low priority since it is already used in the reference champion.
discrepancies.resolution: Closed by E3. log1p helps by +0.0070 (2ch) and +0.0078 (5ch): each draw at/below sigma~0.008, but consistent in SIGN, MAGNITUDE and METRIC DIRECTION across two independent channel counts, and the metric it moves is RMSE (-0.0070/-0.0119) -- i.e. RANGE, exactly what the dynamic-range mechanism predicts. So log is a real small effect, NOT noise. What IS refuted is the predicted interaction: log did not help more at 2ch than at 5ch (0.0070 vs 0.0078), so ILD's log-domain ratio does not make magnitude compression redundant. The effects are additive.

discrepancies.observation: E2 (5ch nolog, composite 1.8646) beats both 2ch cells (1.8854, 1.8784) by 0.014-0.021 -- above the sigma~0.008 floor -- but the win comes ENTIRELY from d1 (0.5938 vs 0.5808/0.5785). Its RMSE is actually WORSE than E1's (1.3207 vs 1.3116) and its ABS_REL is the worst of the three (0.4460 vs 0.4143/0.4211).
discrepancies.why_it_matters: The composite weights d1 at 1/0.46, so a d1 gain of 0.013 alone buys 0.028 of composite -- enough to swamp the RMSE regression. So 'derived interaural cues help' is true ONLY in the d1 sense: the cues make more pixels land within +-25% of truth, while the typical squared error gets slightly larger. That is the profile of a mechanism that improves DIRECTIONAL/angular assignment (which is exactly what ILD and IPD encode: azimuth) without improving absolute RANGE. It is not evidence that the cues help distance estimation.
discrepancies.diagnostic_value: Sharpens I1. If the interaural cues fix azimuth but leave range untouched, then range accuracy is limited by something else -- and time-of-flight resolution (I1) is the physically obvious candidate. It also warns that a composite win can be a single-metric win: never report 'E2 is better' without saying better AT WHAT.
discrepancies.next_action: This is now the decision rule for I1: a temporal-resolution gain MUST appear in RMSE (range), not d1. If it appears in d1 it is not acting through time-of-flight. And since log already buys range by making faint far echoes readable, I1's window-smear mechanism is a DIFFERENT range constraint -- if both hold, a combine study is justified.
discrepancies.resolution: Confirmed and generalised by the full 2x2. The dissociation is clean and reproduces at both log settings: interaural cues move d1 (+0.0153/+0.0141, angle) with rmse flat (+0.0021/-0.0028); log1p moves rmse (-0.0070/-0.0119, range) with d1 flat (+0.0023/+0.0011). Cues fix AZIMUTH, compression fixes RANGE. They fix different failure modes, which is why the effects are additive rather than redundant.

discrepancies.observation: Both 2ch cells peak at epoch 14 of 26 and both peak at exactly 2400.3 MB VRAM.
discrepancies.why_it_matters: The overfitting turn and the memory envelope are properties of the architecture + schedule, NOT of the input representation. This makes epoch count a CONFOUND for every comparison run under the fixed wall-clock budget: any change that slows an epoch silently reduces the epochs that fit, and is penalised for reasons unrelated to its mechanism.
discrepancies.diagnostic_value: Any future structural change that costs wall-clock (e.g. I2's extra attention scale) must report epochs-fit alongside the composite, or its result is uninterpretable.
discrepancies.next_action: fix via I3 (match --epochs to the budget); until then, always log best_epoch and epochs_ran

discrepancies.observation: Predicted depth maps in out/display/qualitative.png are low-frequency blobs showing quasi-periodic banding along azimuth, while the GT is sharp and piecewise-constant. The 5ch predictions carry visibly more azimuthal structure than the 2ch ones.
discrepancies.why_it_matters: The input's width axis is STFT TIME with only 18 distinct frames, nearest-upsampled 28.4x into 512 columns -- a staircase. If the predicted banding period matches that staircase, the model is reproducing an artefact of the interpolation rather than scene geometry, and the nearest-neighbour upsample in _features() (editable research logic) is itself a defect. The extra 5ch structure is consistent with S0's finding that interaural cues buy angular assignment.
discrepancies.diagnostic_value: Distinguishes two very different stories: (a) the model is under-fitting and simply produces blurry output; (b) the representation injects a spatial artefact the model faithfully reproduces. These call for opposite interventions.
discrepancies.next_action: none. Superseded by I6 vs I7.
discrepancies.resolution: The banding is NOT an interpolation artefact. Azimuthal FFT (24 val samples, E3 batvision): no peak at the 18-block staircase frequency k=18 (prominence 0.806, i.e. a dip) nor at its harmonics. What I read as banding is the k=4 component -- the four walls of a room -- which the model OVER-represents (pred/gt 1.94). The real characterisation: the model is a LOW-PASS predictor, holding 97.4% of its azimuthal power in k<=6 versus GT's 75.8%, and only ~5% of GT's power at k>=17. This resolves D4 and spawns two competing explanations, I6 (the objective is 58% low-frequency) and I7 (two microphones under-determine fine azimuth), discriminated by I6's ablation.

discrepancies.observation: E4 (RayDPT) fitted only 5 epochs in the 1-hour budget (713.5 s/epoch) versus batvision's 25 (~130 s/epoch). Its best checkpoint is the LAST epoch and all val metrics were still improving monotonically. Peak VRAM 16.2 GB vs 2.4 GB.
discrepancies.why_it_matters: E4's composite 2.0471 vs E3's 1.8567 CANNOT be read as 'RayDPT is the worse model'. One model converged and then overfit for 12 epochs; the other never reached convergence. Under a wall-clock budget, throughput is silently part of the score. Every RayDPT-vs-batvision statement in this phase must carry epochs_ran, and no RayDPT mechanism can be fairly judged until RayDPT can converge inside the budget.
discrepancies.diagnostic_value: Reframes the research: the first-order problem for RayDPT is not its ray conditioning, its decoder, or its representation -- it is throughput (I8). It also retro-explains why D2 (batvision's overfitting turn at epoch 13-14) and this are the same confound seen from opposite ends: batvision has budget to spare, RayDPT has none.
discrepancies.next_action: I8: bf16 autocast as the minimal pure-speed change; re-measure s/epoch, epochs_ran, best_epoch. Also I9: delete the 16.78M dead encoder params.
discrepancies.resolution: CONFIRMED by E9. A converged RayDPT beats a starved one by 0.1162 (14 sigma). E4's 2.0471 measured budget, not architecture. Every RayDPT number in this phase must carry epochs_ran and best_epoch.

discrepancies.observation: I1's CONTROL arm improved. E5 (win400 hop40) cut RMSE by 0.0227 and the composite by 0.0132 (above sigma) versus E2, despite leaving the analysis window -- and therefore the temporal smear of 1.417 m -- completely unchanged.
discrepancies.why_it_matters: The arm was designed to isolate 'sampling density' from 'resolution' and to show nothing. It showed something, and in RMSE, the very metric reserved as evidence FOR the resolution mechanism. A Nyquist check says hop=160 already samples the window's envelope at 1.2x Nyquist, so arm A added ~no information. Something other than information improved range accuracy.
discrepancies.diagnostic_value: Forces a third explanation: the nearest-neighbour resize (I10). If bilinear at hop=160 recovers the gain, then BOTH E5's reading and I1's original framing were wrong, and the real defect is an interpolation choice buried in _features -- editable research logic that nobody had questioned.
discrepancies.next_action: Confirm with bilinear+hop40. If that beats E5, smoothness and density are complementary and the residual is real.
discrepancies.resolution: Half explained. Holding information fixed and removing only the staircase (E8) recovers 52% of E5's RMSE gain, so interpolation smoothness IS part of why the control arm moved. But it recovers none of E5's COMPOSITE gain, because the smoother input costs d1. So E5's composite win still requires the extra time samples, whose information content Nyquist says is ~nil. The residual mystery: 4x oversampling a band-limited envelope improves the composite by 0.0132 (above sigma) for reasons neither information nor interpolation fully explains. Candidate: optimisation/conditioning of the encoder input, not the representation's content.

discrepancies.observation: The two I1 arms improved the composite through OPPOSITE metrics. Arm A (density only, smear unchanged): rmse -0.0227, d1 -0.0019. Arm B (6.2x finer smear): rmse -0.0093, d1 +0.0067 -- and B's RMSE is worse than A's despite B having vastly better temporal resolution.
discrepancies.why_it_matters: If temporal resolution set range accuracy, B should own RMSE. It does not; A does, and A did not change resolution at all. Meanwhile B, which also sacrifices frequency resolution (win 400 -> 64), buys ANGLE. That inverts the physical story: sharper transients seem to help azimuth cues (ILD/IPD are read across frequency and time), while range accuracy responds to something in the sampling/interpolation of the time axis.
discrepancies.diagnostic_value: Separates 'the score improved' from 'the mechanism is real' -- the composite improved in both arms while the pre-registered mechanism failed in both. The one variable monotone with the score across all three runs is staircase block width, which I10 isolates.
discrepancies.next_action: I10 first. If bilinear at hop=160 recovers arm A's RMSE gain, then A is explained by interpolation and B's d1 gain needs its own explanation (candidate: shorter windows sharpen transients, aiding IPD-based azimuth).

discrepancies.observation: E6 holds 29.7% LESS high-frequency azimuthal power than E2 (0.0232 vs 0.0331) yet has a BETTER d1 (0.6005 vs 0.5938). Separately, removing 58% of the gradient (E7 vs E3) barely changed the spectrum or the composite.
discrepancies.why_it_matters: It breaks the assumption -- mine, unstated until now -- that d1 improves because predictions get sharper. d1 counts pixels within +-25% of truth, and a well-centred smooth field beats a mis-placed sharp one. So the low-pass character of these models may be largely IRRELEVANT to the metric, and 'restore high frequencies' is probably the wrong research goal.
discrepancies.diagnostic_value: Redirects the whole low-pass line of inquiry. The question is not 'why is the prediction blurry' but 'why is the smooth field mis-centred', i.e. what sets per-ray depth accuracy. It also warns that spectral diagnostics, though cheap, do not proxy the metric.
discrepancies.next_action: Stop optimising for spectral sharpness. Keep the spectrum as a MECHANISM probe (I11) rather than a goal.

discrepancies.observation: With BOTH models converged, RayDPT (E9, 1.9308) trails batvision (E3, 1.8567) by 0.0741 -- and 0.0691 of that gap is d1 alone (0.5631 vs 0.5949). RMSE is essentially tied (1.3195 vs 1.3088, contributing 0.0067).
discrepancies.why_it_matters: d1 is the ANGULAR metric: S0 established that the interaural cues (ILD, IPD), which encode azimuth, buy d1 while log1p compression buys rmse. So the ray-conditioned model -- whose entire premise is that depth should be decoded per ray DIRECTION -- is worse at direction than a plain encoder-decoder with a 1x2 bottleneck, while matching it on range. The mechanism is losing exactly where it claims to win.
discrepancies.diagnostic_value: This is the central question of the project, and it is now sharp and measurable. Competing explanations, none yet tested: (a) capacity -- E9 has 5.00M params against batvision's 54.41M; (b) the decode cut -- E9 decodes at 32x64 and upsamples 8x, though I2's oracle says the 32x64 ceiling (d1 0.9578) is nowhere near the achieved 0.5631, so the ceiling is not binding; (c) the cross-attention cut to one layer; (d) ray-conditioning genuinely does not help angular assignment on this task.
discrepancies.next_action: E13/E14 test I13. If the far deciles recover, D9 was never about angles.
discrepancies.e10_result: E10 (ray_cross_layers restored to 2 at decode 32) moved d1 by only +0.0034 -- 12% of the 0.0284 remaining gap to batvision, below the noise floor. BUT E10 did NOT converge: 15 epochs at 232.3 s/epoch, best checkpoint = LAST epoch. Its d1 is a lower bound on a still-learning model, so S4's question is NOT answered: we cannot say the cross-layer cut was free.
discrepancies.what_e10_did_show: With 2 layers, 15 unconverged epochs already beat 21 converged epochs at 1 layer (composite 1.9192 vs 1.9308, above sigma). Capacity helps per epoch; the budget takes it back.
discrepancies.e11_result: PARTIAL. The first CONVERGED 2-layer RayDPT (E11: 2 layers + coarse KV, 23 epochs, best at 22) recovers d1 +0.0079, closing 25% of the 0.0318 gap to batvision. So the angular deficit is PARTLY a budget artefact (H2) and PARTLY not: 0.0239 survives on a converged model. H1 (ray-conditioning does not aid angular assignment here) still stands for the remaining 75%.
discrepancies.diagnostic_result: SPATIAL DECOMPOSITION (utils/diag_d1.py, full val, instrument verified against the training logs) OVERTURNS the framing. The deficit is NOT angular. By azimuth sector it is nearly flat (0.0180-0.0345, std 0.0056 about a mean of 0.0245). By elevation, the FLOOR shows a deficit of 0.0002 -- the two models are identical there. By GT depth it explodes: 0-1 m RayDPT is BETTER (-0.0219); at 7-8 m the deficit is 0.1559; at 8-9 m it is 0.2271 (batvision 0.4023 vs RayDPT 0.1751). Both models under-predict far depth badly (GT 8.5 m -> batvision 5.74 m, RayDPT 4.97 m); RayDPT simply compresses harder.
discrepancies.reframing: d1 is a +-25% RELATIVE threshold, so it is dominated by the far field, where a 3.5 m shortfall is a 41% error. RMSE barely notices because far pixels are rare. So 'RayDPT loses on the ANGULAR metric' was a misreading of what d1 measures. H1 and H4 both assumed an azimuthal story and are mis-specified: the azimuth breakdown does not support either. The live hypothesis is I13 -- the dense MAE drives predictions to the conditional median and compresses the far tail.

discrepancies.observation: Small-sample diagnostics REVERSE their sign. On 60 val samples RayDPT E9's d1 (0.5689) beat batvision's (0.5661); on the full 3543 it is 0.5632 vs 0.5949. The azimuthal spectrum does the same: at N=32 RayDPT holds MORE k=7..32 power than batvision (0.0159 vs 0.0152); at N=3543 it holds LESS (0.0152 vs 0.0214).
discrepancies.why_it_matters: Every spectral diagnostic in this phase (I5's staircase test, I6's refutation, I11's bottleneck test, D4, D8) was computed on 16-24 samples. The conclusions were reported with more confidence than the instrument could support.
discrepancies.resolution: Re-checked on the FULL val set under the eval_lock. Integrity first: raw metrics reproduce the training logs exactly (batvision 1.3088/0.5949, RayDPT 1.3195/0.5632). Then: I11's direction HOLDS (batvision 0.0214 vs RayDPT 0.0152 in k=7..32, so the model without the waist really is smoother). D8's direction HOLDS (E2 0.0262 vs E6 0.0219, -16.4% rather than the -29.7% claimed). I6's refutation never depended on the spectrum -- its decisive evidence was a composite delta of +0.0046 from full-val training runs. So no conclusion is overturned, but three were stated with unearned precision.
discrepancies.policy_change: All diagnostics now run on the FULL val set, on GPU, under the eval_lock. Never conclude from a subset without first checking that the subset reproduces a known full-set number.

discrepancies.observation: E15 (--cross-kv16 e3, otherwise E11's config and lr) DIVERGED at epoch 4: train loss 0.1673 -> 0.3625 -> 0.5764, val d1 0.5087 -> 0.3580 -> 0.3312. The blow-up is entirely in `lc`, the 16x32 coarse-layout term: 0.4402 versus E11's 0.0618.
discrepancies.why_it_matters: lc = masked_mae(D_coarse, gt_c, m_c) with D_coarse a sigmoid, so it is bounded by 1.0. A value of 0.44 means D_coarse has SATURATED, not merely drifted. Routing e3's 2048 tokens into cr16 makes F16 a sum over 4x more attention values; CrossBlock is a residual (q + attn) so magnitudes accumulate, and coarse_head + sigmoid sits directly on m16 = F16 + se4(e4). E11's linear-scaled lr of 1.2e-3 is too large for that path.
discrepancies.classification: OPTIMISATION failure, not 'mechanism unsupported' (program.md's negative-evidence taxonomy). NOTHING is concluded about I14 from it.
discrepancies.diagnostic_value: Warns that the coarse head is the fragile point of RayDPT: it is a sigmoid sitting one 1x1 conv away from the deepest ray-attention output, with no normalisation between them. Any change that enlarges F16 risks it. A LayerNorm before coarse_head is a cheap structural fix worth its own study.
discrepancies.next_action: A proper `tune` study over lr at the fast config, not another architectural guess. The lesson: I named a cause from a correlation (which term spiked most) instead of from an intervention.
discrepancies.e17_confirms: E17 (the fast defaults: win32=3, ffn=2) diverged the SAME way at the SAME lr: lc jumps 0.0633 -> 0.3209 at epoch 5 and never recovers (stuck at 0.5-0.64 for twenty epochs), dragging mae and low with it. Two architecture changes, two identical divergences.
discrepancies.generalisation: At lr 1.2e-3 the three runs that left the architecture alone (E9, E11, E12) were stable; BOTH runs that changed it diverged. E11 survived not because it had margin but because it sat on the edge. So the obstacle to fast experimentation is NOT FLOPs -- it is that every knob turn becomes an lr search. Efficiency work is blocked on stability.
discrepancies.root_cause: coarse_head is Conv2d(dim,1,1) + sigmoid sitting directly on m16 = F16 + se4(e4), with no normalisation. F16 is a residual accumulation over CrossBlocks whose magnitude grows with the KV set and the FFN width. Once the sigmoid saturates its gradient is ~0 and lc cannot recover.
discrepancies.root_cause_CORRECTED: MY ORIGINAL ATTRIBUTION WAS WRONG. I blamed coarse_head because lc spiked hardest. But E18 sets w_coarse_layout=0, so lc contributes NO gradient at all -- and the run still destabilised, at epoch 7, with mae jumping x1.51 (0.0632 -> 0.0953) and `low` x1.52 alongside it. lc is a MEASURING INSTRUMENT reading m16 through a sigmoid, not the cause: when the trunk moves, the sigmoid amplifies it. Deleting the auxiliary delayed the spike from epoch 5 to epoch 7 and did not prevent it.
discrepancies.revised_root_cause: The instability is in the TRUNK, and the only remaining candidate is the optimiser: lr 1.2e-3, obtained by the linear scaling rule from 3e-4 at batch 16. E9/E11/E12 survived it; the two configurations with LESS capacity per layer (win32=3, ffn=2) and the one with a larger KV set (kv16=e3) did not. Gradient clipping is already at 1.0 and does not prevent it.

discrepancies.observation: EchoDelayVolume beat its control by 0.0112 on the fast parent (E24 vs E21) and LOST by 0.0121 on the champion parent (E25 vs E23). Same mechanism, opposite signs, both above sigma.
discrepancies.why_it_matters: program.md flags exactly this -- a mechanism whose outcome depends on its parent. But before crediting the parent, look at the budget: EchoDelayVolume costs 12 s/epoch, and on the slower champion architecture that pushed E25 from 22 epochs to 21, cut its cosine schedule to 81%, and left its best checkpoint at the LAST epoch. E24 survived only because the fast knobs had bought it epochs (best 23 of 24). The apparent parent-dependence may be pure budget.
discrepancies.diagnostic_value: If E27 (same config, --epochs 21 so cosine anneals) converges and beats E23, the parent-dependence dissolves and the mechanism is real. If it converges and still loses, the mechanism genuinely needs the capacity the fast knobs removed -- which would itself be a finding about what the ray pathway spends its capacity on.
discrepancies.next_action: This is evidence about WHERE the far-field capacity lives, not a defect to patch. The productive combine is EchoDelayVolume with the ONE change that gave the fine time tokens directly (kv=e3 routing, I14) -- not with the champion's extra dense capacity.
discrepancies.resolution: PARENT-DEPENDENCE IS REAL, not a budget artefact. E27 (champion + EDE, --epochs 21, cosine fully annealed to lr 0, converged) scored 1.9271 vs E23's 1.8962 -- WORSE than the unconverged E25, and the far deciles it was designed to fix REGRESSED: 8-9 m -0.0362, 9-10 m -0.0230 (against E24's +0.076 and +0.060 on the fast parent). The mean predicted depth at GT 8-9 m FELL from 5.044 m to 4.824 m. So the schedule fix removed the confound and the mechanism still loses on the champion.
discrepancies.mechanism: The two parents differ only in win32 (3 vs 5) and ffn_mult (2 vs 4) -- both are cross_kv32=e4. The champion already does far-field better than the fast model WITHOUT EchoDelayVolume (E23 8-9 m d1 0.2306 vs E21 0.1845). So the wider local attention and larger FFN are ALREADY buying the far-field accuracy EchoDelayVolume targets, and stacking the two makes them compete for the same capacity rather than compose. EchoDelayVolume helps a WEAK model and hinders a strong one.

discrepancies.observation: THREE ways of adding far-field time resolution to a model that already has EchoDelayVolume all FAILED to improve the far deciles: finer volume bins (E29 e2), fine-token routing (E30 kv=e3), and the champion's dense capacity (E27). Yet EchoDelayVolume itself, added to a bare model, DID improve them (E24).
discrepancies.why_it_matters: It is the signature of a SATURATING resource. The first dose of far-field time-resolution structure helps (E24: +0.06-0.08); every additional dose, by any path, does nothing or hurts. So RayDPT's far-field d1 is now limited by something OTHER than how much echo-timing structure the decoder has. The remaining suspects have not been touched: angular observability with two mics (I7), or the ENCODER discarding time before the volume ever reads it -- e3 is the STFT's 512 columns pooled 8x by the encoder, and no amount of downstream structure recovers what the encoder averaged away.
discrepancies.diagnostic_value: Redirects the far-field question from the DECODER (exhausted: E24/E27/E29/E30) to the ENCODER and the REPRESENTATION. If the encoder's pooling is the limit, a volume read from a LESS-pooled feature -- or the raw STFT -- should help where e2 did not. If even that saturates, the limit is the sensor (I7).
discrepancies.next_action: Test whether the ENCODER is the bottleneck: EchoDelayVolume reading a minimally-pooled time axis (e2 was only 2x; try the STFT's own time resolution). If that also saturates, the far field is sensor-limited and the lever moves to the representation (multi-window STFT) or is a genuine ceiling.
discrepancies.resolution: SENSOR-LIMITED, not encoder-limited. E31 removed ALL encoder time pooling (raw STFT, 512 time columns, frequency held at e3's size so time was the only variable) and the far deciles still regressed by ~0.04. Time resolution is not the far-field bottleneck at any stage: representation (STFT hop/window), encoder pooling, volume bins, and token routing were all tested and all saturate past the first dose. The far field is limited by what two microphones can OBSERVE about distant faint echoes (I7), not by how finely the pipeline resolves arrival time.
discrepancies.implication: Every decoder- and encoder-side far-field lever is now exhausted. The lever, if one exists, is the REPRESENTATION (cues carrying more range information -- multi-resolution STFT, interaural coherence) or it is a scoped ceiling to be stated as such.

divergence_checkpoints.evidence_summary: The reference is anchored (E3, 1.8567; cues buy angle, log buys range, additive). RayDPT converged only after a capacity cut (E9, 1.9308) and still trails by 0.0741, of which 0.0691 is d1 -- the ANGULAR metric, where ray-conditioning claims to win (D9). The low-pass character of every model survives a 58% change to the objective (I6 refuted), a 6.2x change in temporal resolution (I1's mechanism refuted), and removal of the network's information waist (I11 refuted). Decoder output resolution is not binding (I2 refuted). The staircase in _features explains half of one RMSE gain and no composite gain (I10 partial).
unexplained: D6: 4x oversampling a band-limited envelope improves the composite by 0.0132 for reasons neither information nor interpolation explains.
D7: the two temporal-resolution arms improved through OPPOSITE metrics, inverting the physical story.
D8: E6 has 29.7% LESS high-frequency power than E2 yet a BETTER d1 -- sharpness is not what d1 rewards.
D9: the ray-conditioned model loses to a plain U-Net almost entirely on ANGLE while tying on RANGE.

competing_hypotheses.h: H1 ray-conditioning genuinely does not aid angular assignment on this task; per-ray queries add cost, not directional information.
competing_hypotheses.test: a CONVERGED 2-layer RayDPT. If d1 stays ~0.566, H1 stands.

competing_hypotheses.h: H2 the angular deficit is a budget artefact: no 2-layer RayDPT has ever converged, so d1 has never been read on a finished model.
competing_hypotheses.test: E11 (kv=e4, 24.7 epochs, same 5.89M params as E10).

competing_hypotheses.h: H3 raw capacity: 5.00-5.89M against batvision's 54.41M. RayDPT may simply be too small.
competing_hypotheses.test: widen dim/ngf to match parameter count at a cost that still converges.

competing_hypotheses.h: H4 angular observability: two receivers under-determine fine azimuth, and BOTH models are already at that limit; batvision's d1 edge comes from something else (e.g. full-resolution decoding).
competing_hypotheses.test: give batvision RayDPT's 32x64-decode handicap and see whether its d1 falls to ~0.566.

competing_hypotheses.h: H5 the DPT skips: RayDPT adds encoder features to the ray grid by pixel index, equating (frequency,time) with (elevation,azimuth). A false correspondence would corrupt exactly the angular assignment. (I5 was parked because its predicted staircase signature was absent, but the axis mismatch was never tested.)
competing_hypotheses.test: ablate se2/se3/se4 on a converged RayDPT; d1 should move if the false correspondence hurts.

competing_hypotheses.h: H6 the ray queries are direction-only: RayBank encodes (x,y,z) + Fourier PE of the DIRECTION, but nothing about the two ears' geometry, so a query cannot ask 'what does a surface at THIS azimuth do to my ILD?'. use_mic_pe exists and is False.
competing_hypotheses.test: enable use_mic_pe (adds +-y, the ear axis) on a converged model; cheap, one flag.

competing_hypotheses.h: H7 evaluation: d1 is a +-25% threshold. batvision's advantage may be a better-calibrated GLOBAL depth scale rather than better angles. Check whether a single per-image affine rescale of RayDPT's prediction closes the d1 gap.
competing_hypotheses.test: zero GPU: fit one scale+shift per image on the val set and recompute d1.

chosen: H7 first (zero GPU, decides whether the deficit is angular at all)
then H2/E11 (the only way to read d1 on a converged 2-layer model)
H6 kept as the cheapest mechanism-side follow-up
divergence_checkpoints.rationale: H7 costs nothing and could dissolve D9 entirely: if a per-image affine rescale closes the d1 gap, RayDPT's problem is a global depth SCALE, not angles, and every angular hypothesis (H1, H5, H6) is misdirected. Run it before spending an hour. H2 is the necessary control -- no 2-layer RayDPT has ever converged, so D9's central claim rests on models that were still learning.
divergence_checkpoints.H7_result: REFUTED on the full val set. An ORACLE per-image scale (median GT/pred ratio) closes only 0.0037 of the 0.0318 d1 gap (0.0318 -> 0.0281). A per-image affine closes 0.0127 but degrades d1 for BOTH models (batvision 0.5949 -> 0.5382), so it is not a fair correction. A global depth-scale miscalibration does not explain D9: the deficit is genuinely angular. D9 survives its cheapest possible dissolution.
divergence_checkpoints.H6_result: REFUTED, zero GPU, by reading RayBank. The premise -- 'a ray query knows its direction but nothing about the ears' -- is false. prepare.py's frame is x=front, y=left(+)/right(-) EAR AXIS, z=up, and RayBank sets use_xyz=True, so every query already carries y, the ear-axis component of its own direction, plus a Fourier PE of it. The `use_mic_pe` flag appends exactly [y, -y] (verified: allclose to y and -y), which is linearly redundant. Query content does not explain the angular deficit.
divergence_checkpoints.status_after_h6_h7: H6 and H7 both refuted at zero GPU cost. E11 shows D9 is 25% budget (H2, partly), 75% not. Surviving: H1 (mechanism), H3 (raw capacity 5.89M vs 54.41M), H4 (angular observability), H5 (DPT skips' false axis correspondence). H1 and H4 make OPPOSITE spatial predictions and are separated by utils/diag_d1.py, which costs one GPU minute after E12.
divergence_checkpoints.H1_H4_status: BOTH MIS-SPECIFIED. Each assumed the d1 deficit is azimuthal; the full-val spatial decomposition shows it is not (flat across azimuth, zero on the floor, exploding with GT depth). H4's sharp prediction -- deficit concentrated in the front/back confusion cones -- is only weakly present (front 0.0345, back 0.0260, side 0.0180) and cannot carry a mean deficit of 0.0245 that exists everywhere. Superseded by I13.

divergence_checkpoints.evidence_summary: Champion E23 (1.8962) trails batvision E3 (1.8567) by 0.0395, and 0.040 of that is d1. The d1 deficit is concentrated in the far field: at 7-10 m RayDPT trails batvision by +0.12 to +0.17 per decile. Three independent results point at fine TIME resolution as the lever: E9-vs-E12 (fine tokens buy +0.0965 of far d1), EchoDelayVolume on a starved decoder (E24, far deciles +0.06-0.08, pre-registered prediction confirmed), and batvision's only structural edge being that its U-Net skips carry full time resolution to the decoder while RayDPT bottlenecks through e4 (16x32). Refuted routes: loss re-weighting (I13), decoder output resolution (I2), the information bottleneck as a LOW-FREQUENCY cause (I11). lr must stay unscaled at 3e-4 (I18).
unexplained: D8: E6 has less high-frequency azimuthal power than E2 yet better d1 -- sharpness is not what d1 rewards.
D7: the two temporal-resolution arms improved through opposite metrics.

competing_hypotheses.h: H8 EchoDelayVolume is time-resolution-starved: it reads e3 (time 64), which is the STFT's 512 columns pooled 8x. Feed it e2 (time 128) for 2x finer delay resolution.
competing_hypotheses.test: E24-style run with the volume reading e2 instead of e3, on the fast parent where the mechanism works.

competing_hypotheses.h: H9 EchoDelayVolume + fine-token routing (I14, kv=e3) are complementary: both feed far-field signal by different paths, and neither is the champion's dense capacity. The scope of I19 predicts they compose where EDE-plus-dense did not.
competing_hypotheses.test: volume + cross_kv32=e3 at lr 3e-4 (kv=e3 diverged at 1.2e-3, D11; now testable).

competing_hypotheses.h: H10 the ray bottleneck itself is the far-field limit: e4 (16x32) throws away the time detail before the rays ever see it. Add an e2/e3-resolution ray-decode stage that batvision-style carries fine time to the head.
competing_hypotheses.test: add a 64x128 ray-decode scale (Q64 + se1). Expensive; screen cost first.

competing_hypotheses.h: H11 far-field failure is data, not architecture: far surfaces are a minority of pixels AND return the weakest echoes, so the model sees them rarely and faintly. A depth-balanced sampler or far-weighted metric-blind augmentation would test it.
competing_hypotheses.test: oversample scenes with far surfaces; recompute far deciles.

competing_hypotheses.h: H12 batvision's edge is NOT time resolution but full-res spatial decoding: it decodes 256x512 directly while RayDPT upsamples x8 from 32x64. Give RayDPT the same by adding a light full-res refinement head.
competing_hypotheses.test: a conv refinement at 256x512 on top of the x8 upsample.

competing_hypotheses.h: H13 the champion already saturates its far-field capacity via win5/ffn4; the real ceiling is angular observability with two mics (I7, still a candidate). Then no decoder change closes the far-field gap and the lever is the REPRESENTATION (more mics' worth of cues, e.g. multi-window STFT).
competing_hypotheses.test: add a second STFT resolution as extra channels; re-check far deciles.

chosen: H8 first (cheapest, directly extends the one confirmed mechanism)
then H9 (the combine the I19 scope predicts)
H10 held as the expensive structural option if H8/H9 stall
divergence_checkpoints.rationale: H8 is the minimal extension of the ONE mechanism whose pre-registered prediction was confirmed: EchoDelayVolume works, and it is currently reading a time axis pooled 8x from the STFT. Doubling its delay resolution (e2, time 128) is nearly free and directly tests whether its scoped far-field gain scales with time resolution -- which is the through-line of every confirmed far-field result. H9 is the combine its own scope predicts. Both run on the FAST parent, where I19 is known to help, isolating the new variable.
